meta_desc: See exact window cleaning prices for Salmon Arm homes. Learn what affects your quote — window count, interior vs exterior, add-ons — and book online today.
h1: Window Cleaning Cost in Salmon Arm: What You'll Actually Pay
intro: Most homeowners in Salmon Arm have no idea what window cleaning costs until they're standing on a ladder in May, squinting into the pollen-smeared sun. Transparent pricing is rare in this industry — so here's exactly what Wagon Windows charges and why, broken down by home size and service type.

sections.h2: Wagon Windows Pricing for Salmon Arm Homes
sections.body:
Wagon Windows uses flat-rate pricing based on home size rather than a per-window count that fluctuates with every measurement. This means you get a firm number before anyone shows up at your door, and the price doesn't change unless the scope does.

For residential exterior-only cleaning, pricing starts at $169 for a small home (roughly under 1,400 sq ft), $229 for a medium home (1,400–2,200 sq ft), and $299 for a large home over 2,200 sq ft. Interior and exterior combined starts at $229 for small homes. Gutter cleaning starts at $149 for single-storey and $229 for two-storey properties.

Screen cleaning is priced per screen — typically $4–5 per screen — and track cleaning is available as an add-on. Hard water stain removal is quoted separately because severity varies too much to flat-rate: a minor mineral haze and a years-old calcium deposit require entirely different treatment.
bullets: Exterior only (small, under 1,400 sq ft): $169
Exterior only (medium, 1,400–2,200 sq ft): $229
Exterior only (large, 2,200+ sq ft): $299
Interior + exterior (small): from $229
Gutter cleaning (single-storey): $149
Gutter cleaning (two-storey): $229
Screen cleaning: $4–5 per screen
Hard water stain removal: custom quote
sections.body2: These prices reflect the Salmon Arm market and the actual time it takes to do the job properly. Low-ball quotes that surface on community boards often omit screen cleaning, skip tracks entirely, or charge extra for anything above ground level.

sections.h2: What Affects the Price of Window Cleaning on Your Specific Home
sections.body:
Three factors move a window cleaning quote more than anything else: the number and type of windows, whether you want interior service, and accessibility.

Window count matters, but it's not just about quantity. Large picture windows take more time than a standard double-hung sash. Windows above the roofline that require a ladder setup at an awkward angle add time. Bay windows and specialty shapes (arched, octagonal, transom sets) require more care than a standard rectangle.

Interior service adds roughly 40–60% to an exterior-only price, depending on home size. It also requires more coordination — furniture gets moved, screens come out and are cleaned separately, and the cleaner needs access to every room. If you only want interior cleaning on the main floor or in specific rooms, that can be scoped separately.

Accessibility drives cost more than most people expect. A single-storey rancher in Salmon Arm's west end is straightforward. A home built into a hillside with a daylight basement — common in the Broadview or Canoe areas — may require 30-foot ladders or pole systems to reach what looks like a second-storey window from the back. Homes with extensive landscaping or decks that block ladder placement take longer to set up safely.
bullets: Window count and size
Interior vs exterior scope
Height and ladder access requirements
Add-ons: screens, tracks, hard water treatment

sections.h2: Seasonal Demand and Booking Lead Times
sections.body:
Salmon Arm's window cleaning season follows a predictable pattern, and that pattern directly affects how far in advance you need to book.

Spring — specifically late April through May — is the busiest window for the entire year. Cottonwood pollen coats everything from roughly the third week of April through early June. Most homeowners want their windows cleaned after the pollen settles, which creates a hard rush period where every available cleaning slot fills within days. If you're calling in mid-May asking for a same-week appointment, you'll likely be disappointed or waiting until June.

Summer brings a second wave of demand, particularly for lake-adjacent properties in Blind Bay, Sorrento, and along the Shuswap Lake shoreline. Mineral spray from the lake and summer dust accumulation push homeowners to book a mid-season clean before hosting season peaks in July and August.

Fall is more flexible. September and October are typically easier to schedule, though the window closes fast once deciduous trees start dropping leaves and homeowners combine window cleaning with gutter service before freeze.

Winter service is limited to above-freezing days — exterior cleaning requires temperatures above 5°C to prevent streaking and freezing runoff. Booking is easier but availability depends on weather windows.

The practical advice: book spring service in March or early April. Book fall service before mid-September. Calling within a week of when you want the job done is feasible in summer and fall, but nearly impossible in peak spring.

sections.h2: Why Transparent Pricing Matters for Salmon Arm Homeowners
sections.body:
The window cleaning industry has a persistent problem with bait-and-switch pricing. A company advertises a low entry price, arrives on-site, and then adds charges for screens, for the second storey, for 'excessive buildup', or for any number of other items that should have been in the original quote. By the time the work is done, the bill is double what was quoted.

Transparent pricing matters for a few reasons specific to Salmon Arm. First, many homeowners in the Shuswap have lake-facing windows with genuine hard water mineral buildup — a problem that absolutely should be disclosed and quoted separately, but it shouldn't be used as a surprise line item on invoices for homes that don't have that issue. Second, a significant portion of Salmon Arm's housing stock is older or has irregular window configurations (common in properties that were built or expanded incrementally over decades), which means 'standard' assumptions about window count often don't apply.

Wagon Windows provides quotes before showing up so you know exactly what you're paying. The online booking system lets you describe your home upfront, and any ambiguity gets resolved over the phone before a booking is confirmed — not when the invoice is in your hand.

sections.h2: Getting the Most Accurate Quote for Your Salmon Arm Home
sections.body:
To get the most accurate quote without surprises, a few pieces of information help: approximate square footage or the number of bedrooms (a useful proxy), whether you want interior service, whether you have screens and want them cleaned, and whether you know of any hard water staining on the glass.

For homes in areas with known mineral issues — particularly those within a few hundred metres of the lake or those on well water with high iron content — mentioning this upfront allows for an accurate scope. Hard water stain removal is a separate service from a standard clean, and quoting it as part of a bundle avoids the awkward mid-job discovery conversation.

If your home is on a steep lot or has unusual window placement (skylights, floor-to-ceiling glass, clerestory windows), photograph or describe it when booking. Homes in Salmon Arm's hillside neighbourhoods often look like single-storey from the street and reveal a full daylight basement from the back — a detail that affects both labour time and equipment needs.

Call (403) 837-5092 or use the online booking form at wagonwindow.com. Most quotes can be confirmed same-day for standard residential jobs.

faqs.q: How much does window cleaning cost for an average Salmon Arm home?
faqs.a: An average Salmon Arm home in the 1,400–2,200 sq ft range runs $229 for exterior-only or from $299 for interior and exterior combined. Homes outside that range are priced up or down accordingly. Screens and gutter cleaning are separate add-ons.

faqs.q: Does the price change if my home has hard water stains?
faqs.a: Yes. Standard window cleaning removes dirt and grime but won't eliminate mineral deposits bonded to the glass. Hard water stain removal is a separate service quoted based on severity and number of affected windows. It costs more, but it's far cheaper than replacing glass.

faqs.q: Is interior window cleaning included in the base price?
faqs.a: No. The base price covers exterior only. Interior cleaning is available as an add-on or as a combined interior-exterior service, which starts at $229 for small homes. Most customers doing a full seasonal clean choose the combined option.

faqs.q: How far in advance do I need to book for spring cleaning?
faqs.a: Spring (late April through May) fills fastest. Booking in March or early April is the best way to secure your preferred date. If you're calling in May, expect availability to be limited to late May or June.

meta_title: Window Cleaning Prices BC 2026 | Wagon Windows
meta_desc: Compare window cleaning prices across BC in 2026. See how Interior BC and Shuswap rates compare to the Lower Mainland — and what red flags signal a cheap, bad quote.
h1: Window Cleaning Prices in BC for 2026: What to Expect Across the Province
intro: Window cleaning prices in BC vary more than most people realize — not just between cities, but within regions, depending on what's actually included in the quote. If you're pricing out service in the Shuswap and comparing quotes you've seen from Vancouver contractors or seen discussed online, the numbers won't translate directly.

sections.h2: What Window Cleaning Typically Costs Across BC in 2026
sections.body:
Across British Columbia, residential window cleaning for a typical single-family home runs roughly $150–$400 for an exterior-only service, with the range driven by home size, region, and what's included in the scope. Interior-and-exterior combined service runs $250–$600+ for larger or more complex homes.

Those ranges are wide because BC's housing stock is wildly diverse: a post-war 900 sq ft bungalow in Prince George is nothing like a 4,000 sq ft new-build in South Surrey. Professional companies price based on time and equipment, and the inputs differ dramatically between a compact rancher and a multi-level home with skylights and specialty glass.

Per-window pricing — which some companies advertise — tends to run $8–$15 per window exterior-only in most BC markets. Screens add $3–$6 per screen depending on size. These per-unit prices can seem attractive on simple homes but often miss hidden complexity that a flat-rate quote already accounts for.
bullets: Lower Mainland (Metro Vancouver): $180–$500+ for residential exterior
Okanagan / Interior BC (Kelowna, Vernon): $150–$350 exterior
Shuswap region (Salmon Arm, Blind Bay): $169–$299 exterior
Northern BC (Prince George, Kamloops area): $130–$280 exterior
Screen cleaning province-wide: $3–$6 per screen
sections.body2: These ranges reflect honest, professional service — not the bottom of the market. What you'll pay at the very low end of online quote boards typically reflects incomplete scope, unlicensed operators, or quality that creates the need for a redo.

sections.h2: Lower Mainland vs Interior BC: Why Prices Differ
sections.body:
Window cleaning in Metro Vancouver costs more than in Interior BC for straightforward economic reasons: labour costs, overhead, and competitive market density all price higher in the Lower Mainland. A cleaning company operating in Vancouver is paying more for insurance, fuel, and labour than one operating out of Salmon Arm or Kamloops.

But the Lower Mainland's higher baseline price doesn't necessarily mean better quality. Interior BC operators often serve smaller markets where reputation travels further and repeat business matters more. In Salmon Arm, word-of-mouth in a city of 20,000 people is powerful in a way it simply isn't in a metro of 2.5 million. Operators who cut corners don't last.

One major difference that pushes Interior BC prices down slightly: simpler housing stock on average. Many Shuswap homes are single-storey or modest two-storey builds without the architectural complexity common in newer Lower Mainland subdivisions. That said, the Shuswap's lake-proximate properties introduce their own complexity — hard water mineral deposits, algae on frames, and hillside lots that require longer ladder setups.

sections.h2: What Shuswap Window Cleaning Prices Actually Include
sections.body:
In the Shuswap market, a legitimate quote from a professional operation should include: cleaning all exterior glass surfaces up to the quoted storey height, squeegee-finishing every pane, and wiping frames and sills dry. Screens are typically priced separately — a good company will tell you this upfront rather than leaving you to discover it later.

Wagon Windows includes frame wipe-down with every clean. Tracks are a priced add-on. Screens are $4–5 per screen. Hard water stain removal is quoted separately because severity varies so much: a light haze on a lakeside window is a different job from calcified deposits that have been accumulating for five or six summers.

Downspout flushing and condition reporting come with gutter cleaning ($149 for single-storey, $229 for two-storey). These aren't extras — they're part of a complete gutter service because a cleaned gutter with a blocked downspout is still a failed gutter.

Knowing what's included means you can make an accurate price comparison. A $120 quote that doesn't include screens or sill wiping is not a better deal than a $169 quote that does — it's a lower number for less work.

sections.h2: Red Flags in Cheap BC Window Cleaning Quotes
sections.body:
Cheap quotes in the BC window cleaning market follow predictable patterns. Knowing the warning signs protects you from paying twice.

The biggest red flag is extreme underpricing relative to home size. If a quote for a two-storey, 2,400 sq ft home comes in under $120 for exterior cleaning, something is being cut: scope, time, insurance, or all three. Labour alone for a legitimate two-storey exterior clean on that size home runs well over two hours, which means a sub-$120 price implies either a very low wage or a very fast (incomplete) job.

Quotes with vague scope language — 'windows cleaned,' 'full house service' without any specifics — are a signal that the price will expand on arrival. Always ask before booking: does this include screens? What height is covered? Are sills wiped? How are hard water deposits handled?

Cash-only operations with no online presence, no reviews, and no verifiable business address warrant caution in any home service category. In window cleaning specifically, working at height creates liability that uninsured operators push onto you as the homeowner if something goes wrong.

Unlicensed operations also tend not to carry WCB coverage, which is relevant if a worker is injured on your property. A small price difference is not worth that exposure.
bullets: Extreme underpricing for home size (under $120 for a full 2-storey exterior)
Vague scope that expands on arrival with surprise line items
Cash-only, no reviews, no verifiable business address
No confirmation of insurance or WCB coverage

sections.h2: Getting a Fair Price for Your Shuswap Home
sections.body:
The cleanest way to get a fair price in the Shuswap is to describe your home accurately when booking and ask explicitly what the quote includes. Size (approximate square footage or number of bedrooms and storeys), whether you want interior service, screen count, and any known hard water issues give a professional company everything needed to quote accurately.

For Salmon Arm, Blind Bay, Sorrento, Sicamous, Chase, Enderby, and Armstrong properties, Wagon Windows serves the full region and can quote any location in the Shuswap. Prices don't inflate based on distance within the service area — a Blind Bay booking is quoted the same way as a Salmon Arm one.

Call (403) 837-5092 or book via our booking page at wagonwindow.com. You'll get a confirmed price before the booking is locked — no surprises on invoice day.

faqs.q: Are window cleaning prices in Salmon Arm cheaper than in Kelowna or Vancouver?
faqs.a: Generally yes. Interior BC labour and overhead costs are lower than Metro Vancouver, and Kelowna's larger market supports slightly higher pricing than smaller Shuswap communities. That said, the price difference is more moderate than many expect — perhaps 15–30% less than comparable Vancouver service for a standard home.

faqs.q: What's typically not included in a BC window cleaning quote?
faqs.a: Screen cleaning, track cleaning, interior service, and hard water stain removal are commonly excluded from base quotes. Always ask before booking. A professional company will tell you explicitly what's in and out of scope — vague answers are a red flag.

faqs.q: Is there a travel surcharge for properties outside Salmon Arm?
faqs.a: Wagon Windows serves the full Shuswap — Blind Bay, Sorrento, Sicamous, Chase, Enderby, Armstrong, and surrounding communities — without a travel surcharge within the service area. Confirm coverage for your specific location when booking.

faqs.q: Why are some online quotes for BC window cleaning under $100?
faqs.a: Sub-$100 quotes for full residential service almost always involve incomplete scope, no insurance, or both. They often don't include screens, second-storey access, or frame cleaning. Paying once for a complete, insured job is better value than paying a low rate for a partial clean — then paying again to fix what was missed.

meta_title: How Many Windows Does My House Have? | Wagon Windows
meta_desc: Learn how to count windows for a cleaning quote. Understand panes vs units vs openings, typical counts by home size, and how to give an accurate number when booking.
h1: How Many Windows Does My House Have? A Guide for Getting Your Quote Right
tags: how many windows does my house have
intro: When you call to book window cleaning, the first question is often about window count — and most homeowners haven't thought about it. The way you count matters, because different companies use different counting methods, and a misaligned definition can lead to quote surprises.

sections.h2: Windows vs Panes vs Openings: Getting the Terminology Right
sections.body:
The word 'window' means different things depending on who you ask, and in window cleaning, the difference affects your quote. Three terms come up constantly: units, panes, and openings.

A window unit is the full assembly in a rough opening — frame, sash, and glass. A double-hung window is one unit with two panes (upper and lower sash). A sliding window is one unit with two panes side by side. A picture window is one unit with one pane.

A pane is a single piece of glass. That double-hung window has two panes. A three-panel sliding door has three panes.

An opening refers to the hole in the wall. One rough opening may hold a single picture window or a combination unit with multiple sashes and panes.

When you see per-window pricing advertised, it usually means per pane or per unit — and which one matters enormously. A home with fifteen window units that each have two sashes has thirty panes. Per-unit and per-pane pricing for that home would produce very different totals.

Wagon Windows uses flat-rate pricing by home size rather than per-window counting, which sidesteps this ambiguity entirely. But understanding the terminology helps when comparing quotes from multiple companies.
bullets: Unit: the full window assembly (one rough opening)
Pane: a single piece of glass within a unit
Opening: the hole in the wall that holds the unit
sections.body2: A safe default when someone asks your window count: count each sash (movable glass panel) as one window. A standard double-hung counts as two. A casement with one sash counts as one.

sections.h2: Typical Window Counts by Home Size
sections.body:
Most homeowners are surprised by how many windows their home actually has once they walk through and count. Here are typical ranges for BC residential homes:

Small homes (under 1,400 sq ft, 2–3 bedrooms): typically 12–20 panes. These are often ranchers or compact two-storeys with standard window sizes and no specialty glazing.

Medium homes (1,400–2,200 sq ft, 3–4 bedrooms): typically 20–35 panes. This category is the most common in Salmon Arm's established neighbourhoods — the 1970s–2000s builds that make up the bulk of the housing stock in the Canoe, South Broadview, and downtown-adjacent areas.

Large homes (2,200+ sq ft): typically 35–60+ panes. New builds in particular tend toward larger, more numerous windows. Homes with great room windows, clerestory windows above main floor ceilings, or floor-to-ceiling lake-view glass can push counts well above this range.

Lake-view and custom homes in the Shuswap often skew toward the high end for their square footage category — large picture windows oriented toward Shuswap Lake are a design staple, and those homes frequently have 50+ panes despite modest floor plans.
bullets: Small home (under 1,400 sq ft): 12–20 panes
Medium home (1,400–2,200 sq ft): 20–35 panes
Large home (2,200+ sq ft): 35–60+ panes
Custom / lake-view homes: often 50+ panes regardless of size

sections.h2: How to Walk Through and Count Your Windows
sections.body:
Counting your own windows takes about five minutes and prevents miscommunication at booking. Start outside and walk the perimeter of your home, counting every glass pane visible from outside. Don't forget the back of the garage, utility room windows, or basement windows that may be partially below grade.

Then go inside. Walk each room and count the panes in every window. Check rooms you rarely use — spare bedrooms, home offices, laundry rooms, bathrooms. Bathroom windows are easy to miss and are often worth cleaning because they're near the shower and collect soap film on the inside.

Note any windows that aren't accessible from outside at normal ladder height. Skylights are a separate category — they require roof access and are typically scoped separately. Windows above vaulted ceilings that are only reachable with a 30-foot ladder are in a different bracket from standard second-storey windows.

Also note the screens. Each window screen is a separate item for cleaning purposes. Screens that have been in place through multiple pollen seasons in Salmon Arm can be coated in cottonwood fluff and fine organic matter that a garden hose won't remove — professional screen cleaning with a soft-brush wash and rinse makes a visible difference.

sections.h2: Why Accurate Window Count Affects Your Price (and Your Experience)
sections.body:
Accurate window counts aren't just about fairness in pricing — they affect scheduling. A cleaner arriving for a job quoted at 20 windows who discovers 40 panes, plus screens, plus an accessible second storey, can't simply absorb that difference without extending the job or returning for a second visit.

For flat-rate services like Wagon Windows, home size is the primary pricing input, so exact pane counts matter less than the overall scope. But describing your home accurately — including any unusual features like a daylight basement that adds an extra 'storey' of accessible windows from the back, or a large addition — helps ensure the flat-rate size bucket is correct.

Any time a quote feels surprisingly low for your home's size, ask how many windows it's priced for and what happens if the actual count is higher. A professional answer is 'we price by home size, here's what that includes, and here's how we handle exceptions.' A concerning answer is vagueness or 'we'll figure it out on the day.'

faqs.q: Does a sliding glass door count as a window for cleaning purposes?
faqs.a: Usually yes. Sliding glass doors have glass panes that collect the same dirt and mineral deposits as windows, and they're cleaned the same way. Some companies count each pane of a sliding door separately — confirm when getting a quote.

faqs.q: Do skylights get cleaned with regular window cleaning?
faqs.a: Skylights require roof access and are typically quoted separately. Not all window cleaning companies service skylights. Ask specifically when booking if you have them.

faqs.q: My home has a suite — does that double the window count?
faqs.a: An in-law suite or basement suite adds windows that may or may not be included in a standard quote, depending on accessibility and whether the suite has separate exterior access. Mention the suite when booking and confirm the scope covers it.

faqs.q: Can I just tell the company my home's square footage instead of counting windows?
faqs.a: Yes — square footage (or bedroom/bathroom count) is often the easiest proxy, and it's how flat-rate companies like Wagon Windows structure their pricing. Pair that with any notable features (lake-view glass, extra storeys, known hard water) for the most accurate quote.

cta_text: Not sure of your window count? Describe your home and we'll size it right — call (403) 837-5092 or book at wagonwindow.com.

meta_title: Is Professional Window Cleaning Worth It? | Wagon Windows
meta_desc: Honest answer: is hiring a pro window cleaner worth the cost? We cover time savings, safety risks, streak-free results, and when DIY is actually fine for BC homeowners.
h1: Is Professional Window Cleaning Worth It? An Honest Assessment for BC Homeowners
tags: is professional window cleaning worth it
intro: The honest answer is: it depends on your home. For a single-storey rancher with accessible windows and twenty minutes to spare, DIY window cleaning with the right tools is perfectly reasonable. For a two-storey home in Salmon Arm with hard water staining, a ladder-access back deck, and cottonwood pollen covering everything — that's a different calculation entirely.

sections.h2: The Real Time Cost of DIY Window Cleaning
sections.body:
Most homeowners significantly underestimate how long it takes to clean their windows to a streak-free finish. A medium-sized home with 30 panes inside and out, plus screens, takes an experienced professional roughly 3–4 hours. An inexperienced homeowner working with a bucket, a squeegee they've never used properly, and multiple trips up and down a ladder is looking at 6–8 hours — or more, if they're also dealing with streaks that require a second pass.

That time cost is real. At a professional rate of $229–$299 for a full exterior clean on a medium-to-large Salmon Arm home, you're paying roughly $60–75 per hour for someone who brings the right tools, the right technique, and no learning curve. Whether that's worth it is a personal calculation, but most people who try DIY once and then hire a professional don't go back.

The technique gap matters more than it sounds. Professional squeegee work leaves glass truly streak-free because the tool is maintained, the solution is correct, and the stroke technique is consistent. Consumer squeegees from hardware stores are usually adequate; the limitation is almost always technique, not equipment. YouTube can teach the basics, but the first few windows will demonstrate the gap between watching and doing.

sections.h2: Ladder Safety: The Argument That Actually Closes the Case
sections.body:
Falls from ladders are the leading cause of serious home injury for adults in Canada. Working on an 8-foot stepladder to reach a standard second-storey window is manageable for most people. Working on a 20-foot extension ladder on uneven ground, while leaning out to reach a window on a hillside lot, while holding a bucket and a squeegee — that's a genuinely dangerous activity for anyone without training and the right equipment.

Shuswap homes built on slopes present a particular challenge. What looks like a single-storey home from the street can have a full daylight basement exposed at the back, effectively creating 25+ feet of working height from natural grade. Homes in the Broadview and hillside neighbourhoods of Salmon Arm, and many lake-view properties in Blind Bay and Sorrento, have exactly this configuration.

Professional window cleaners work at height routinely and carry the appropriate equipment: commercial extension ladders, standoff brackets for roof overhangs, and in some cases water-fed pole systems that eliminate ladder work on accessible surfaces entirely. They also carry liability insurance that covers them if something does go wrong — a protection you don't have if you're the homeowner on the ladder.

This isn't fear-mongering. Experienced homeowners climb ladders safely all the time. But one unlucky step during a window cleaning session creates medical and financial consequences that dwarf the cost of hiring out the job.
bullets: Falls from ladders are the #1 cause of serious home injury for adults in Canada
Second-storey work on hillside lots can reach 20–25 feet
Professional cleaners carry insurance; homeowners don't if they're the one who fell

sections.h2: Results: What Pro Cleaning Actually Achieves vs DIY
sections.body:
On a straightforward single-storey window with no mineral buildup, a careful DIYer with the right technique can achieve results comparable to a professional. The gap narrows considerably on accessible, standard windows where time and technique are the only variables.

The gap widens dramatically in three situations: hard water staining, screens, and anything requiring ladder access at height.

Hard water staining — caused by mineral deposits bonded to the glass surface — cannot be removed by standard cleaning. It requires acid-free treatment products designed to dissolve calcium and silica deposits without damaging the glass. Homeowners who try to scrub mineral deposits off with standard glass cleaner typically either fail to remove them or damage the glass with abrasives. In the Shuswap, lake-adjacent properties and homes on well water with high mineral content are common candidates for this treatment.

Screens cleaned by spraying with a garden hose look cleaner but aren't. The fine mesh traps pollen, dust, and organic matter that hosing doesn't dislodge. Professional screen cleaning with a soft-brush wash removes the layer that's actually blocking airflow and contributing to streaky glass.

At height, professional results beat DIY almost universally — because the professional can position safely, take their time, and use the right technique. A DIYer on an unstable ladder is focused on not falling, not on squeegee angle.

sections.h2: When DIY Is Actually Fine
sections.body:
Being honest about this matters: there are situations where DIY window cleaning is the sensible choice.

If your home is a single-storey rancher with all windows accessible from ground level, you have a good squeegee, the right cleaning solution, and you're comfortable with the technique — there's no strong reason to hire out the work. A standard exterior clean on those terms is a 60–90 minute job.

If you clean frequently (every 6–8 weeks during the growing season) and your windows don't have mineral buildup, the maintenance level of dirt is light enough that a quick DIY pass keeps them presentable between professional cleans. Some customers use Wagon Windows for the spring deep clean — which includes screens, tracks, and a thorough first-pass after cottonwood season — and then maintain with DIY touch-ups through summer.

What DIY doesn't replace is the periodic professional deep clean that catches buildup before it becomes a damage problem, the safety advantage on anything above ground floor, and the time savings on whole-house cleans that genuinely take most people most of a day.
bullets: Single-storey, ground-accessible windows: DIY is reasonable
Frequent light maintenance between pro cleans: perfectly sensible
Multi-storey, hard water, cottonwood buildup: hire a professional

sections.h2: Amortizing the Cost: What Professional Cleaning Costs Per Year
sections.body:
A full-service professional window clean on a medium Salmon Arm home — exterior clean, screens, and tracks — runs roughly $229–$280. If you do this twice a year (spring post-pollen and fall pre-freeze), that's $460–$560 annually.

Spread across 12 months, that's roughly $40–$47 per month to have professionally cleaned windows year-round. For most homeowners who think carefully about it, that number is smaller than expected.

Consider also what it costs not to clean properly. Mineral deposits that aren't treated eventually etch into the glass permanently. Screens blocked with pollen and organic matter corrode faster. Window frames that aren't wiped down accumulate grime that accelerates paint failure and seal degradation. The cost of regular professional cleaning is, in part, preventive maintenance — cheaper by a wide margin than the glass replacement or repainting it defers.

faqs.q: How much time does it realistically take to clean windows yourself on a medium-sized BC home?
faqs.a: Budget a full day for a medium home (30+ panes inside and out, plus screens), including setup, cleaning, and dealing with any streaks on the first pass. If you're inexperienced with a squeegee, add more time. A professional completes the same job in 3–4 hours.

faqs.q: Can I remove hard water stains myself with store-bought products?
faqs.a: Some light mineral haze responds to white vinegar or hardware-store calcium removers. Severe deposits — especially from Shuswap Lake spray or high-mineral well water — typically need professional-grade acid-free treatments applied with specific technique. Abrasive attempts to scrub them off can scratch glass permanently.

faqs.q: Is professional window cleaning safer than DIY on a two-storey home?
faqs.a: Meaningfully so. Professional cleaners have the right ladders, training, and insurance for working at height. Falls from residential ladders cause thousands of serious injuries annually in Canada. For second-storey work, the risk reduction is a genuine argument for hiring out.

faqs.q: How often do I actually need professional window cleaning in the Shuswap?
faqs.a: Most Shuswap homeowners get the best results from three professional cleans: late April/May after cottonwood, mid-summer for dust and pollen, and September before freeze. Lake-adjacent properties may benefit from more frequent service due to mineral spray.

cta_text: Ready to hand off the ladders and the squeegee? Book professional window cleaning at wagonwindow.com or call (403) 837-5092.

meta_title: Window Cleaning Cost Per Window BC | Wagon Windows
meta_desc: Understand per-window window cleaning pricing in BC. Learn why per-window rates can be misleading, what drives real cost, and how add-ons like screens and tracks factor in.
h1: Window Cleaning Cost Per Window: What the Per-Window Model Actually Means
intro: You've probably seen window cleaning advertised at a price per window — $8 per window, $12 per window, $15 per window. It sounds like a simple, transparent way to price. The reality is that per-window pricing is often one of the least transparent ways to quote window cleaning, and knowing why helps you compare quotes accurately.

sections.h2: How Per-Window Pricing Works — and Where It Gets Complicated
sections.body:
Per-window pricing charges a set rate for each window cleaned, usually differentiated by side (interior vs exterior) and sometimes by height or size. On the surface, it's simple: count your windows, multiply by the rate, get a price.

The complication starts with definitions. As covered in our guide to window counts, a 'window' can mean a pane, a unit, or an opening — and the distinction matters. A double-hung window (two sashes) that a company counts as two panes at $8 each costs $16. Another company might quote that same window as one unit at $15. Neither is wrong — they're using different definitions. But a homeowner comparing those quotes assuming the same unit is being priced will misjudge the comparison.

Per-window pricing also tends to get more complex with every variation: large windows, specialty shapes, windows above a certain height, sliding glass doors (one unit or three panes?), skylights. Each variation is typically a pricing tier or a surcharge, and the additions accumulate. A quote that starts at $8 per window for a standard single-hung can look very different by the time it's applied to a home with large picture windows, a few dormer windows in the roof, and a glass wall in the dining room.

sections.h2: What Per-Window Prices Look Like in Practice
sections.body:
In the BC residential market, per-window rates for exterior cleaning typically run:

$6–$10 per window for standard-sized windows, exterior only, ground or first floor. $10–$15 for second storey. $15–$20+ for large picture windows or windows requiring special equipment. Interior adds roughly the same amount again per window as exterior.

Applying those rates to a medium Salmon Arm home with 28 panes (exterior only, mix of storeys and sizes), you'd likely land in the $200–$280 range before any extras. That's comparable to flat-rate pricing for the same home — which is part of the point: for a standard home, well-priced per-window and flat-rate quotes converge on similar totals.

The divergence happens at the extremes. A home with many small, simple, accessible windows — think a compact single-storey with standard double-hung windows — costs noticeably more under per-window pricing than a well-priced flat rate. A home with fewer but very large, complex, or high windows may cost more under a flat rate (if the flat rate is set by home size and doesn't account for window complexity) than under a time-honest per-window quote.
bullets: Standard exterior, ground level: $6–$10 per window
Second-storey exterior: $10–$15 per window
Large picture windows: $15–$20+ per window
Interior typically matches or slightly exceeds exterior per-window rate

sections.h2: Add-On Costs That Apply Regardless of Pricing Model
sections.body:
Whether a company quotes per window or by flat rate, certain services are almost always priced separately because they represent genuinely separate scope.

Screen cleaning is the most common add-on. In the Shuswap, screens clog with cottonwood pollen, insect debris, and fine organic matter through the spring and summer. Cleaning them properly requires removing each screen, washing the mesh and frame, and reinstalling. Wagon Windows charges $4–5 per screen. Most companies in BC run $3–6 per screen depending on size.

Track cleaning is another separate service. Window tracks — the channel in the frame where the sash slides — accumulate dead insects, dirt, and moisture that standard window cleaning doesn't address. Track cleaning involves brushing and vacuuming the tracks and wiping them down, which takes additional time. It's worth doing when tracks are visibly dirty or when windows are sticking.

Hard water stain removal is always a separate line item because the severity varies enormously. A light haze from a single summer's mineral exposure is a 20-minute treatment on a standard window. Years of accumulated lake spray on 15 windows facing Shuswap Lake is a multi-hour job requiring multiple treatment passes. Lumping this into a per-window rate would either overcharge clean windows or underprice badly stained ones.
bullets: Screen cleaning: $3–6 per screen (Wagon Windows: $4–5)
Track cleaning: typically quoted as an add-on
Hard water stain removal: custom quote based on severity and count

sections.h2: Flat Rate vs Per Window: Which Is Better for Shuswap Homeowners
sections.body:
For most Shuswap residential properties, flat-rate pricing by home size produces more predictable costs and fewer invoice surprises. You know the number before anyone shows up, and it doesn't change because the professional counts your panes differently than you did.

Per-window pricing works best when the home has an unusual configuration where complexity falls outside the normal range for its size — either very simple (few, small, accessible windows in a large house) or very complex (many specialty windows in a small footprint). In those cases, per-window pricing more accurately reflects actual time on-site.

Wagon Windows uses flat-rate pricing tied to home size, with explicit separate pricing for screens, gutters, and hard water treatment. This model is transparent because the add-on prices are published and the base rate doesn't shift based on how panes are counted on the day.

When comparing quotes from multiple companies, the most useful exercise is to ask each company: what does this price include, what does it exclude, and what would change the price after you arrive? That question exposes the actual scope of each quote far better than comparing the headline number.

faqs.q: Is it cheaper to hire a company that charges per window vs flat rate?
faqs.a: It depends on your home. For a standard medium home, the totals typically converge. Flat-rate pricing is more predictable. Per-window can be cheaper for very simple homes and more expensive for homes with large or complex windows. Compare by asking both types of companies to quote your specific home.

faqs.q: Does screen cleaning cost extra under per-window pricing?
faqs.a: Almost always yes. Screen cleaning is a separate operation from window cleaning and is priced additionally by virtually all companies, whether they quote per window or flat rate. Expect $3–6 per screen in BC.

faqs.q: My home has a mix of large and small windows. Which pricing model is better?
faqs.a: For mixed window sizes, flat-rate pricing by home size often wins because it averages across the mix. Per-window pricing can penalize homes with many large windows. Ask both types of companies to quote your home before deciding.

faqs.q: What does 'per side' mean in per-window pricing?
faqs.a: 'Per side' means the quoted rate applies to one face of the glass — either interior or exterior. A per-side rate of $8 means $8 for exterior-only per window, $16 for both sides. Confirm whether a quoted per-window rate is per side or per full unit.

cta_text: Get a flat-rate quote with no per-window surprises — book at wagonwindow.com or call (403) 837-5092.

meta_title: Gutter Cleaning Cost Shuswap | Wagon Windows
meta_desc: See Shuswap gutter cleaning prices from $149. Learn what's included, why twice-yearly cleaning matters in the Shuswap, and what skipping it actually costs your home.
h1: Gutter Cleaning Cost in the Shuswap: Pricing, What's Included, and Why It Matters
intro: Gutters are one of those maintenance items that feel optional until they fail — and when they fail, the bill is not a gutter cleaning bill. It's a fascia replacement bill, a foundation repair bill, or a basement remediation bill. Regular gutter cleaning in the Shuswap is cheap prevention against expensive consequences.

sections.h2: Shuswap Gutter Cleaning Prices: What Wagon Windows Charges
sections.body:
Wagon Windows prices gutter cleaning at $149 for single-storey homes and $229 for two-storey homes in the Shuswap. These are flat rates that apply across the service area — Salmon Arm, Blind Bay, Sorrento, Sicamous, Chase, Enderby, Armstrong, and surrounding communities.

What's included in the flat rate: all gutters cleared of debris (leaves, cottonwood seed pods, conifer needles, organic matter), downspout flushing to clear blockages and confirm flow, and a basic condition report noting any visible damage to gutters, fascia, or downspout connections. The condition report isn't a formal inspection, but it's a professional eye on the state of your drainage system — useful information about problems that need attention before water damage sets in.

What's not included: gutter repairs, gutter guard installation, or remediation of existing water damage. If cleaning reveals a section of gutter pulling away from fascia or a cracked downspout elbow, that's flagged in the condition report and is a separate repair. Knowing about those issues early is the point — catching a loose gutter bracket before it fails is a $40 hardware store fix; missing it until the gutter pulls away and directs water down the siding is an insurance claim.
bullets: Single-storey gutter cleaning: $149
Two-storey gutter cleaning: $229
Included: debris removal, downspout flush, condition report
Not included: repairs, gutter guard installation, water damage remediation

sections.h2: Why Shuswap Gutters Need Cleaning Twice a Year
sections.body:
Two cleanings per year is the right cadence for most Shuswap properties, and the local ecosystem makes a strong case for it.

Spring, specifically late April through June, is cottonwood season. Cottonwood trees are among the most prolific seed-shedders in the Shuswap and across Interior BC generally. The white seed fluff they produce doesn't just drift across your windshield — it packs into gutters, compresses when wet, and creates dense mats that block water flow within weeks of the trees releasing. A spring clean in late May or early June, after cottonwood season peaks, clears these mats before summer rain events.

Fall, September through October, is the deciduous leaf-drop. Birch, maple, and deciduous trees common in Salmon Arm's established neighbourhoods deposit large volumes of leaf material. Wet leaves pack solid in gutters, retain moisture through winter, and create ideal conditions for wood rot in the underlying fascia. A fall clean before the first hard freeze protects the drainage system through the winter period when it's inaccessible.

Conifers — Ponderosa pine and Douglas fir are abundant across the Shuswap — shed needles year-round but particularly in late summer. Properties surrounded by conifers may benefit from a mid-summer check, though for most homes the twice-yearly schedule catches the significant accumulations.
bullets: Spring (May–June): cottonwood fluff packs gutters solid
Fall (Sept–Oct): deciduous leaf drop before freeze
Conifers: year-round needle shed, peaks in late summer

sections.h2: What Happens When Shuswap Gutters Don't Get Cleaned
sections.body:
The consequences of blocked gutters are well-documented and genuinely expensive. Understanding them makes the $149–$229 annual or biannual cost feel different.

Overflowing gutters don't just look bad — they dump water at the foundation. Salmon Arm gets sufficient summer rainfall and spring snowmelt that a single blocked-gutter overflow event during a heavy rain can direct hundreds of litres of water against the foundation. Over years, this causes foundation cracking and basement moisture intrusion. In older Shuswap homes — many built in the 1960s through 1980s with foundations that weren't designed for modern drainage expectations — this risk is meaningful.

Water overflowing behind the gutter soaks the fascia board. Fascia rot typically develops slowly and invisibly until it's extensive. By the time it's visible, replacement is often required on large sections. Fascia replacement on a two-storey Shuswap home runs $800–$3,000+ depending on extent and access. A $149 annual gutter clean is objectively better use of the money.

Blocked downspouts add another failure mode: water that can't exit through the downspout backs up in the gutter and eventually overflows in the gutter section above the blockage, often at an uncontrolled point away from any drainage control.

In Shuswap winters, blocked gutters fill with standing water that freezes. Ice dams add weight that pulls gutters from fascia and can force water under shingles at the eave. Cleaning gutters in the fall prevents the conditions that create spring ice dam damage.

sections.h2: Combining Gutter Cleaning with Window Cleaning for a Better Deal
sections.body:
Many Shuswap homeowners book gutter cleaning and window cleaning together in the spring and again in the fall. There's a practical efficiency to this: a cleaning crew is already at the home, equipment is set up, and the combined booking reduces total scheduling overhead.

Wagon Windows offers both services and can combine them in a single visit. The spring combination — post-cottonwood window cleaning plus gutter clearing after the seed fluff has settled — is the most popular schedule for the Salmon Arm area. It takes care of the home's exterior maintenance in a single booking before hosting season.

The fall combination (window clean before the mud and leaf-fall season, gutter clean after peak leaf-drop) works similarly well, though timing is slightly trickier: you want to clean gutters after most leaves have fallen but before the first hard freeze, which gives a window of roughly late September through mid-October in the Salmon Arm area.

For properties in Blind Bay, Sorrento, and the Shuswap Lake shoreline areas, the spring combination is particularly important because lake-facing windows accumulate mineral spray through the ice-off period, and gutters on lake-adjacent properties tend to accumulate algae and organic debris from the moist lakeside environment.

sections.h2: Gutter Cleaning for Shuswap Properties with Complicated Roof Lines
sections.body:
Not all gutters are equally accessible. Standard single-storey gutters are serviced quickly from a 6-8 foot ladder. Two-storey gutters, gutters above attached garages, and gutters on homes with steep roof pitches require more equipment and time.

The Shuswap has a high proportion of properties with complex roof lines — steep-pitched A-frames, chalet-style lake properties, homes with wrap-around verandas and continuous gutters at multiple levels. These properties still require two cleans per year; the equipment just needs to match the access.

Wagon Windows services these properties as part of the standard two-storey rate where applicable, or by custom quote for configurations outside the standard bracket. When booking, describe your home's storeys, any attached structures with separate gutters (garages, covered decks), and any unusual roof pitch that you're aware of.

Homes on hillside lots in Salmon Arm present a version of the two-storey problem: the rear of the home accesses the roof at a different effective height than the front. Book as a two-storey if your back gutter height from grade exceeds standard single-storey reach.

faqs.q: How long does gutter cleaning take for a typical Salmon Arm home?
faqs.a: A single-storey home typically takes 45–90 minutes. A two-storey with longer gutter runs, downspout flushing, and debris removal runs 1.5–3 hours. Homes with heavy cottonwood buildup or multiple attached structures take longer.

faqs.q: Is the downspout flush always included?
faqs.a: Yes, for Wagon Windows. Downspout flushing is part of the standard gutter clean because a cleared gutter that drains into a blocked downspout isn't a solved problem. The flush confirms the full drainage path is clear.

faqs.q: Can I combine gutter and window cleaning in one booking?
faqs.a: Yes, and it's a common booking choice for spring and fall maintenance. Call (403) 837-5092 or book at wagonwindow.com and specify both services — the crew brings the right equipment for both.

faqs.q: My gutters overflow during rain but look clean from the ground — what's happening?
faqs.a: A blocked downspout is the most common culprit. Gutters can look empty from below while a packed debris plug in the downspout prevents drainage. The gutter fills and overflows upstream of the blockage. A professional flush will clear it.

cta_text: Book your spring gutter clean before cottonwood season peaks — call (403) 837-5092 or book at wagonwindow.com.

meta_title: Screen Cleaning Cost BC 2026 | Wagon Windows
meta_desc: BC screen cleaning costs $4–5 per screen at Wagon Windows. Learn what's included, when professional cleaning beats a garden hose, and the pollen problem in the Shuswap.
h1: Screen Cleaning Cost in BC: What to Expect and When It's Worth It
intro: Window screens are the most overlooked part of a window cleaning job. Homeowners spray them with a garden hose, they look clean from a distance, and that's where the attention ends. In the Shuswap, that approach leaves a layer of cottonwood pollen and fine organic matter packed into the mesh — and that layer matters more than it looks.

sections.h2: Screen Cleaning Prices in BC: What You'll Pay
sections.body:
In BC, professional window screen cleaning typically runs $3–6 per screen, with most quality operators in Interior BC — including the Shuswap — landing in the $4–5 range. The price per screen varies with size: standard window screens at the lower end, large patio door screens or oversized specialty screens at the higher end.

Wagon Windows charges $4–5 per screen as an add-on to window cleaning service. A medium Salmon Arm home with 20 screens would add $80–$100 to a window cleaning bill — a modest cost relative to the difference it makes in appearance and airflow.

Low-end operators often advertise screen cleaning as 'free' or 'included' — and then deliver exactly the level of care that implies: a quick spray that moves dirt around without removing it. Professional screen cleaning involves removing each screen from the window, washing the mesh and aluminum frame with a soft brush and cleaning solution, rinsing, and reinstalling. That process can't be rushed and can't be meaningfully free as part of a bundle.
bullets: BC market range: $3–6 per screen
Wagon Windows: $4–5 per screen
Large patio door screens: typically at the upper end of the range
Average medium home (20 screens): $80–$100 add-on

sections.h2: What Professional Screen Cleaning Actually Involves
sections.body:
Professional screen cleaning is a three-step process: remove, wash, reinstall. Each step has a right and wrong way.

Removal means taking screens out of the window frame without bending the corners or distorting the mesh tension. Screens that have been in place for years can be brittle at the corner splines, and screens from older Shuswap homes often have aluminum frames that have begun to oxidize, making them slightly stiff in their channels. Forcing them causes damage; the right technique is patient leverage at the frame corners.

Washing means scrubbing the mesh on both faces with a soft-bristle brush and an appropriate cleaning solution — not high-pressure spray, which distorts the mesh weave and can pop the spline from its channel. The frame channels and corners collect the most debris and need specific attention. After scrubbing, a thorough rinse removes the loosened material completely rather than redistributing it.

Reinsertion requires aligning the screen properly in its channel and pressing it flush. Screens reinstalled crookedly allow insects in at the gap and look poor from inside. Taking the extra 30 seconds to seat a screen correctly is the difference between a professional result and a DIY result.

sections.h2: The Shuswap Pollen Problem: Why a Garden Hose Isn't Enough
sections.body:
Cottonwood pollen is the most visible of the Shuswap's allergens, but the pollen problem for window screens is more complex than the white fluff implies. Fine particulate from cottonwood, birch, and alder trees — all common across the Salmon Arm and Shuswap Lake area — loads into window screens through the spring growing season.

Under a magnifying glass, a 'clean-looking' screen that's been hosed down once reveals a layer of fine particulate in the mesh weave that the hose rinse didn't reach. That layer does two things: it reduces airflow through the screen (measurably, in well-used windows), and it acts as a substrate for secondary buildup. Pollen, dust, and moisture bond into the existing organic layer, creating a progressively denser blockage that accumulates season by season.

For homes with anyone sensitive to pollen or respiratory irritants, this buildup has real air quality implications — particularly on windows that are frequently open in spring and early summer. Removing that layer with a proper brush wash rather than a spray rinse produces a different result.

For homes near Shuswap Lake, there's an additional factor: fine mineral particulate in lake air deposits on screens and contributes to a hard-to-clean layer that's part mineral, part organic. These screens benefit from professional cleaning even when they look acceptable from a distance.

sections.h2: Aluminum Frame Corrosion in Lake-Adjacent Shuswap Homes
sections.body:
Aluminum screen frames on properties near Shuswap Lake face a specific challenge: mineral-rich air accelerates the oxidation process that produces the chalky white powder (aluminum oxide) visible on older frames. This isn't just cosmetic — oxidized aluminum is slightly porous, which makes it harder to clean and more prone to retaining organic buildup.

For lake-adjacent properties in Blind Bay, Sorrento, Sicamous, and along the Shuswap Lake shoreline, frames that have been in place for 5+ years without cleaning often show visible oxidation at the corners and along the frame channels. Professional cleaning removes surface oxidation and the debris it traps, but frames that are significantly corroded may need replacement to maintain seal integrity.

The practical recommendation for lake-proximity homes: clean screens twice yearly (spring and fall) to slow oxidation progression. Annual cleaning is adequate for most Salmon Arm inland homes. Inspect frame corners for cracking or distortion; a frame that's pulling apart at the corners or has visible corrosion pitting should be replaced rather than cleaned — a damaged frame allows insects past the mesh regardless of how clean the screen surface is.
bullets: Annual cleaning for most inland Shuswap homes
Twice-yearly for lake-adjacent properties in Blind Bay, Sorrento, Sicamous
Inspect frame corners: cracking or corrosion pitting = replace, not just clean

faqs.q: Can I clean my window screens myself instead of paying for professional cleaning?
faqs.a: Yes, for light maintenance. Lay the screen flat, scrub gently with a soft brush, dish soap, and water, then rinse thoroughly. What DIY can't match is the systematic approach to heavy cottonwood buildup or mineral-loaded screens — for those, professional cleaning removes material that a household rinse leaves behind.

faqs.q: Do screen prices vary based on size?
faqs.a: Yes. Standard window screens are at the lower end of the range ($4). Large patio door screens, oversized picture-window screens, and custom sizes run higher ($5–6). When booking, mention if you have patio door screens or oversized screens.

faqs.q: Are screens cleaned at the same time as windows?
faqs.a: They can be — and that's the most efficient way to schedule it. Wagon Windows cleans screens as an add-on during a window cleaning visit. The screens come out, get washed while the windows are cleaned, and are reinstalled before the crew leaves.

faqs.q: My screens look fine. Do they actually need professional cleaning?
faqs.a: If they've been in place through a full Shuswap spring without being properly washed, they almost certainly have a layer of fine pollen and organic matter in the mesh weave that isn't visible from a normal viewing distance. Whether that matters to you depends on airflow preferences and sensitivity to allergens.

cta_text: Add screen cleaning to your next window cleaning booking — call (403) 837-5092 or book at wagonwindow.com.

meta_title: Hard Water Stain Removal Cost BC | Wagon Windows
meta_desc: Hard water stain removal is custom-quoted based on severity. Learn what drives cost, why Shuswap Lake mineral spray causes it, and how treatment compares to glass replacement.
h1: Hard Water Stain Removal Cost: What Drives the Price and What to Expect
intro: Hard water stain removal is one of the few window cleaning services that can't be flat-rated, and the reason is straightforward: a light mineral haze from one season and a five-year calcium crust from lake spray are two entirely different jobs requiring different time, products, and sometimes multiple treatment passes.

sections.h2: Why Hard Water Stain Removal Is Always a Custom Quote
sections.body:
Standard window cleaning removes dirt, grime, and environmental buildup from glass surfaces. It does not remove mineral deposits — calcium, magnesium, and silica compounds — that have bonded to the glass. These are chemically different problems that require different treatments.

Most professional window cleaning companies, including Wagon Windows, quote hard water stain removal separately because the severity range is too wide to flat-rate. A homeowner with a single window facing Shuswap Lake that has developed a light haze over one summer needs a 15–20 minute treatment on that window. A home with 12 lake-facing windows that haven't received stain treatment in four years may require multiple treatment passes, time to let the treatment work, and a follow-up visit to confirm the glass is fully clear.

The custom quote process involves assessing the number of affected windows, the severity of the deposits (light haze, moderate calcium buildup, or severe etching from long-term accumulation), and whether the glass has been treated previously. Severely stained glass that's never been treated may need two or three treatment cycles to clear completely, which affects both time and cost.

In the Shuswap, hard water staining is common enough that it warrants a specific conversation when booking any window cleaning service. Don't assume it'll be handled within a standard clean — confirm explicitly.
bullets: Light haze (one season, 1–3 windows): lowest cost tier
Moderate buildup (2–4 seasons, multiple windows): mid-range quote
Severe etching or long-term deposits: highest cost, may require multiple passes
Number of affected windows × severity level = the quote drivers

sections.h2: Shuswap Lake Mineral Spray: The Primary Cause in This Region
sections.body:
Hard water staining in the Shuswap comes from two primary sources: Shuswap Lake mineral spray and well water with high mineral content.

Shuswap Lake water carries dissolved calcium carbonate and other minerals. When wind-driven spray contacts glass and evaporates, it leaves those minerals behind as a residue. One evaporation cycle leaves a barely-visible film. Ten cycles per week across a Shuswap summer produces a noticeable haze. Five years of this, with no treatment, produces a white-grey crust that's visible from across the room.

Properties within 200–300 metres of the lake shoreline — in Blind Bay, Sorrento, along the Shuswap Lake shore east of Salmon Arm, and in Sicamous — are most affected. Windows facing the water and exposed to prevailing wind directions are affected most severely, but even protected windows accumulate over time.

The second source is well water, which is common in rural and semi-rural Shuswap properties. High-iron well water leaves orange-brown staining. High-calcium well water leaves the classic white calcium crust. Both are treatment-responsive but may require different products. Homes that use sprinkler systems drawing from a mineral-rich well can develop staining on ground-level windows from the spray pattern — a source many homeowners don't initially connect to the staining they see.
bullets: Lake spray: dissolved calcium carbonate deposits on exposed glass
Well water irrigation: mineral-rich spray from sprinklers on ground-level windows
Both respond to acid-free treatment but may require different product formulations

sections.h2: The Treatment Process: What Acid-Free Stain Removal Involves
sections.body:
Professional hard water stain removal uses acid-free chemical treatments — typically products based on oxalic acid chemistry or proprietary glass-safe formulations — that dissolve mineral deposits without damaging the glass surface. This is important: the wrong product (harsh acids, abrasive compounds) can etch the glass permanently, converting a treatable condition into an irreversible one.

The process typically follows this sequence: the glass is cleaned of surface dirt first, then the treatment solution is applied and worked into the deposit with a soft applicator. The solution dwells on the surface for a specified time to allow the chemistry to work, then is rinsed thoroughly. For light deposits, this single pass is often sufficient. For moderate to heavy deposits, the process may be repeated immediately or on a follow-up visit.

Heavy or long-standing deposits may require two to four treatment passes before the glass is clear. Some very old deposits include silica etching — the glass surface itself has been chemically altered by years of mineral contact — which may not be fully reversible. An honest professional will tell you if etching is present and set realistic expectations about achievable results. In those cases, treatment improves the appearance significantly but may not return the glass to factory-clear condition.

After treatment, applying a glass sealant that creates a hydrophobic barrier slows future mineral adhesion — the water beads off rather than sitting and evaporating. This is worth asking about for lake-facing windows on Shuswap properties.

sections.h2: Hard Water Stain Removal Cost vs Window Replacement: The Math
sections.body:
Window glass replacement in BC runs $150–$400+ per window for standard double-pane units, depending on size, glass type, and whether the full unit or just the glass is replaced. Custom or specialty glass runs higher. A home with 12 stained lake-facing windows facing replacement would cost $1,800–$5,000+ for the glass alone, plus installation.

Professional hard water stain removal on those same 12 windows — even at a moderate-to-severe severity level requiring multiple treatment passes — typically costs a fraction of that. Even a high-end stain removal quote for a full home with significant staining runs several hundred dollars, not several thousand.

The caveat is the irreversibility threshold noted above: glass that has been severely etched by long-term mineral contact cannot be fully restored by chemical treatment. For glass in that condition, replacement may genuinely be necessary to restore clarity — but the number of windows in that state on any given property is usually small compared to the total affected.

The practical implication is clear: address hard water staining early, before etching develops. Annual or biannual treatment on affected windows prevents the progression from treatable mineral buildup to irreversible etching. The cost difference between early and late treatment is significant.
bullets: Window glass replacement: $150–$400+ per window
Hard water treatment for full home (moderate severity): typically $100–$400
Glass sealant after treatment: slows future mineral adhesion, worth asking about
Prevention (annual treatment) is far cheaper than late-stage restoration

sections.h2: What to Tell Wagon Windows When Booking Stain Removal Service
sections.body:
When calling or booking online, the information that helps produce an accurate hard water stain removal quote: how many windows are affected, how long the staining has been present (rough estimate — years helps more than months), whether the home is lake-adjacent or on well water with known mineral content, and whether the staining is white/grey (calcium) or orange-brown (iron).

If you're not sure of the severity, describing what you see helps: 'a slight haze when light hits it at a certain angle' versus 'a clearly visible white crust on the glass' are different severity signals. If you can share a photo when booking, it's the fastest way to get an accurate quote.

For lake-adjacent Shuswap properties — particularly those in Blind Bay, Sorrento, and the shoreline areas of Sicamous — hard water treatment on the lake-facing windows should be part of the annual maintenance conversation rather than a special request. The conditions that create it are consistent, and treating it preventively is the lowest-cost path.

faqs.q: Can regular window cleaning remove hard water stains?
faqs.a: No. Standard window cleaning removes surface dirt and grime. Hard water mineral deposits are chemically bonded to the glass and require specific acid-free treatments to dissolve. Attempting to scrub them off with standard cleaners or abrasives can scratch the glass.

faqs.q: How do I know if my windows have hard water staining vs just being dirty?
faqs.a: Hard water stains remain visible after washing. They appear as white or grey haze, white crusty deposits, or orange-brown streaks depending on the mineral type. If the staining is still there after a standard clean, it's mineral buildup, not surface dirt.

faqs.q: Is hard water stain removal a one-time fix or an ongoing treatment?
faqs.a: If the mineral source is still present (lake proximity, mineral irrigation), staining will return. Treatment removes existing deposits; a glass sealant applied afterward slows future buildup. Annual or biannual treatment is the realistic maintenance cadence for lake-adjacent Shuswap properties.

faqs.q: Can all hard water stains be removed?
faqs.a: Most mineral buildup responds well to professional treatment. Glass that has been severely etched by years of untreated mineral contact may not restore to full clarity — the glass surface itself has been altered. Treatment still improves appearance significantly, but very old staining may have permanent components.

cta_text: Have hard water stains on your Shuswap windows? Get a custom quote — call (403) 837-5092 or book at wagonwindow.com.

meta_title: How Often Clean Windows BC | Wagon Windows
meta_desc: How often should BC homeowners clean their windows? The Shuswap's pollen, dust, and leaf seasons drive an optimal 3-clean schedule. Lake proximity changes the math.
h1: How Often Should You Clean Your Windows in BC? The Shuswap Schedule Explained
tags: how often should I clean my windows
intro: The standard advice — clean your windows twice a year — is reasonable for much of BC. In the Shuswap, it's the minimum. The combination of cottonwood pollen in spring, summer dust and lake spray, and fall leaf matter creates three distinct accumulation events that each leave a visible residue on glass. A three-clean schedule isn't upselling; it's a direct response to the local environment.

sections.h2: BC-Wide Recommendation: Why Twice a Year Is the Starting Point
sections.body:
For most BC homeowners — particularly in urban or suburban settings where major environmental stressors are moderate — two professional window cleans per year is the standard recommendation. Spring and fall align with the two transition periods when glass accumulates the most: spring catches winter grime and early pollen; fall catches summer dust, cottonwood (for Interior BC locations), and pre-winter organic matter.

This frequency maintains windows in acceptable condition without over-scheduling. For homes with modest window counts, no significant mineral issues, and a location away from agricultural dust, heavy tree cover, or lake spray, two cleans a year produces consistently presentable windows.

What 'twice a year' doesn't account for is regional specificity. Lower Mainland homes face different conditions than Interior BC homes — more rainfall (which does some of the work for you on vertical glass) and different biological load. Shuswap homes face conditions that push the optimal frequency higher.
bullets: Lower Mainland: 2 cleans/year is typically sufficient
Okanagan (Kelowna, Vernon): 2–3 cleans, particularly if near agricultural operations
Shuswap region: 3 cleans recommended for most homes
Commercial properties: quarterly or more (see commercial section)

sections.h2: The Shuswap's Three-Season Accumulation Pattern
sections.body:
The Shuswap's specific combination of tree species, seasonal weather patterns, and agricultural proximity creates three distinct accumulation events that each merit a clean:

Spring (April–June): Cottonwood pollen season. Cottonwood trees are prolific across Salmon Arm and the Shuswap Lake shoreline. The white seed fluff begins in late April and peaks through May into early June. It coats windows, loads into screen mesh, and mixes with fine pollen from birch and alder to create a dense layer on glass. A post-cottonwood clean in late May or early June removes this layer and starts the summer season with clear glass.

Summer (July–August): Dust and lake spray. Summer brings dry, dusty conditions across Interior BC. Agricultural operations near Chase, Enderby, and Armstrong generate fine particulate that travels on summer winds. Combined with lake spray on Shuswap-facing properties and the general haze of a hot dry Interior BC summer, glass that was cleaned in May needs attention again by mid-summer, particularly for lake-view properties.

Fall (September–October): Leaf matter and pre-winter organic residue. Deciduous trees shed fine surface matter before and during leaf drop — a fine organic film that builds on glass from mid-September. Cleaning in late September or October gives the home clean windows going into winter, which is particularly relevant for south and west-facing glass that gets raked by low winter sun angles.
bullets: Spring clean: late May–early June, after cottonwood peak
Summer clean: July–early August, before hosting season peaks
Fall clean: late September–mid October, before first freeze

sections.h2: How Lake Proximity Changes the Frequency Recommendation
sections.body:
For properties on or near Shuswap Lake — in Blind Bay, Sorrento, along the east and south arms of the lake, and in Sicamous — the recommended clean frequency is higher than for Salmon Arm inland properties.

The reasons are the same as those driving hard water staining: lake water carries dissolved minerals, wind-driven spray deposits them on glass, and each evaporation cycle adds to the accumulation. On a wind-exposed lakefront property in Blind Bay, a spring clean may be partially undone within four to six weeks by summer lake spray.

For lake-adjacent homes, four cleans per year is defensible — spring, midsummer, late summer, and fall — particularly if large picture windows face the water. The economics still work: $229–$299 per clean on a medium lake home adds up to $900–$1,200 annually, against the cost of hard water etching that requires treatment or glass replacement.

An alternative for lake properties is three professional cleans with interim DIY spot maintenance on the most-exposed windows between visits. A daily or weekly wipe of lake-facing glass with a squeegee and clean water prevents mineral accumulation without the cost of full professional visits.
bullets: Lake-adjacent (within ~300m of shore): 3–4 professional cleans/year
Severe exposure (directly lakefront, wind-exposed): consider 4 cleans
Interim DIY maintenance on lake-facing glass between professional visits is cost-effective

sections.h2: Commercial Window Cleaning Frequency vs Residential
sections.body:
Commercial properties in Salmon Arm — storefronts, professional offices, restaurants, hospitality businesses — require more frequent window cleaning than residential homes. The reasons are customer perception, higher traffic generating more proximity grime, and regulatory cleanliness expectations in food service environments.

Most commercial storefronts in Salmon Arm's downtown area benefit from monthly exterior cleaning during the operating season (April–October) and bimonthly through winter. High-traffic retail like restaurants and cafes on the main commercial strip often benefit from weekly cleaning, as hand smudges, exhaust, and foot-traffic spray accumulate visibly within days.

Hospitality businesses — hotels, Airbnbs, vacation rentals — have a different frequency driver: guest arrival. Windows cleaned the week before a rental season opens matter more than adherence to a calendar schedule. Wagon Windows works with commercial clients on schedules tailored to their operations.

For commercial properties near agricultural dust sources (relevant for businesses in Enderby and Armstrong particularly), the summer frequency may need to be higher to maintain a presentable street presence during peak tourist season.

sections.h2: Annual Scheduling Strategy for Shuswap Homeowners
sections.body:
The most effective scheduling approach for Shuswap residential homeowners is to plan all three cleans at the start of the year, securing preferred dates in advance rather than trying to book reactively after accumulation is already visible.

Spring fills fastest and books earliest. March or early April is the right time to lock in a late May or early June spring clean. By the time cottonwood is coating everything and you're motivated to call, the good dates are gone.

Summer is more flexible but still benefits from advance booking, particularly for lake-view properties where demand is higher. Target late July for a summer clean if you're hosting or have an upcoming reason to want the windows looking their best.

Fall scheduling can happen in August or September. The target window for fall cleaning is September 15–October 15 in Salmon Arm — late enough for most leaf matter to have fallen from deciduous trees, early enough to complete before reliable freezing temperatures make exterior cleaning impractical.

Call (403) 837-5092 or book all three visits via our booking page at wagonwindow.com. Setting up recurring annual service means you don't have to think about it — the dates are in the calendar and the work gets done.
bullets: March–April: book spring clean (late May–early June slot)
June–July: book summer clean (late July slot)
August–September: book fall clean (Sept 15–Oct 15 slot)

faqs.q: Is twice a year enough for a Shuswap home?
faqs.a: Twice a year is a minimum. For most Shuswap homes — particularly those with any tree cover, lake exposure, or proximity to agricultural dust — three cleans (spring, summer, fall) produces noticeably better results year-round. Lake-adjacent homes benefit from four.

faqs.q: What's the minimum frequency to prevent hard water etching on lake-facing windows?
faqs.a: Annual treatment is the minimum for lake-adjacent windows. Biannual is better. The goal is removing mineral deposits before they have multiple seasons to bond to the glass permanently.

faqs.q: Does rain clean windows enough to reduce professional cleaning frequency?
faqs.a: Not in the Shuswap. Interior BC's summer is too dry for meaningful rain cleaning during the peak accumulation months. Spring rain can temporarily improve appearances while still leaving mineral residue. Rain on dirty windows creates streaks, not clean glass.

faqs.q: Can I schedule multiple cleans per year with Wagon Windows in advance?
faqs.a: Yes. Booking all three annual cleans at once is the easiest way to secure preferred dates — particularly for spring, which fills earliest. Call (403) 837-5092 or book via wagonwindow.com.

cta_text: Book all three cleans for the year now — lock in your preferred dates before spring fills up. Call (403) 837-5092 or visit wagonwindow.com.
