meta_title: Professional Squeegee Technique for Windows Explained
meta_desc: Why does professional squeegee technique produce streak-free windows? The single-pass method, blade quality, and edge detailing—explained in full detail.
h1: Professional Squeegee Technique: Why Pros Get Streak-Free Windows and DIYers Don't
intro: Streak-free windows look simple to achieve and turn out to be surprisingly hard to produce consistently. The difference between a professional window cleaner who finishes a pane in 30 seconds with zero residue and a homeowner who spends 10 minutes getting acceptable results comes down to one thing: technique. Here's exactly what professional squeegee technique involves and why it works.

sections.h2: The Single-Pass Technique vs. Back-and-Forth Wiping
sections.body:
The most fundamental distinction between professional and amateur window cleaning technique is the direction of water management. Professionals move water off the glass in a single controlled direction. Amateurs typically move it back and forth.

The professional single-pass or fan technique works like this: after the T-bar applicator has spread cleaning solution evenly across the pane, the squeegee is positioned at the top of the glass with the blade at an angle—typically 30–45 degrees from horizontal. The first stroke pulls the squeegee across the top of the pane, drying a horizontal band. The blade is then repositioned to overlap the dry top band by about half an inch, and the next stroke pulls across the next band. The overlap catches any water that crept above the blade on the previous stroke. Each pass is a single direction pull—the blade never reverses across wet glass.

At the end of each stroke, the squeegee is lifted cleanly from the glass, the water is flicked off the blade onto a cloth, and the next stroke begins. The blade never drags back through the dry area it just created.

The back-and-forth scrubbing approach—wiping right, then left, then right again—re-introduces dirty water to clean glass on every reverse stroke. Even when the water itself is clean, the direction change deposits water in the wake of the blade. On small panes or with very clean water and fresh rubber, this can produce acceptable results. On large panes or any situation with mineral content in the water, it consistently produces streaks.

Learning the single-pass technique well enough to apply it reliably on all pane sizes and orientations takes significant practice. The hand pressure, blade angle, and stroke speed all interact, and the correct combination varies with rubber type, glass texture, and solution viscosity.

sections.h2: Rubber Blade Quality and Its Effect on Results
sections.body:
The rubber blade is the highest-leverage component in the squeegee system. A worn or low-quality blade is the single most common cause of streaks, even in experienced hands.

Professional squeegee rubber has specific properties that make single-pass technique work. The edge must be sharp and uniform—any nicks, gouges, or rounded sections create gaps in the water film that leave visible streaks. The rubber hardness must match the working temperature and solution type: harder rubber for warm conditions and standard solutions, softer rubber for cold weather when harder rubber becomes too stiff to maintain contact with the glass surface.

All rubber degrades with use. A new rubber edge is a clean, straight cut; after cleaning hundreds of panes, the edge develops micro-deformations that produce fine chatter marks or uneven water removal. Professional cleaners flip their rubber when one edge dulls—each blade has two usable edges—and replace the entire rubber regularly. In a busy commercial operation, rubber might be replaced every few weeks; for residential work, a well-maintained rubber lasts longer but still has a finite useful life.

Consumer squeegees sold in hardware stores typically use rubber of unknown compound hardness, manufactured to a retail price point rather than a performance standard. This rubber often produces adequate results on small bathroom mirrors but fails noticeably on large residential panes.

The practical implication: a professional with premium rubber and correct technique is not using the same tool as a homeowner with a $15 squeegee. The apparent similarity of the tools obscures the actual performance difference.
bullets: Premium rubber (Ettore, Pulex, Sorbo) has consistent hardness and a sharp, uniform edge
Rubber hardness must match working temperature—wrong hardness causes chattering
Both edges of each rubber blade are usable—professionals flip and replace on schedule
Consumer rubber produces acceptable small-pane results; it fails on large residential glass

sections.h2: Edge Detailing: The Step Most DIYers Skip
sections.body:
A pane cleaned with perfect squeegee technique still has a thin bead of water at the perimeter—where the blade couldn't reach the very edge of the glass against the frame. If this bead is left to dry, it leaves a visible water line around the border of every pane. Edge detailing is the step that removes this bead and completes the cleaning.

Professional edge detailing is done with a lint-free cloth—either a professional linen scrim or high-quality microfibre. The cloth is folded to a clean section, pressed to the edge of the glass at the top corner, and drawn down and along the frame in a single wipe that absorbs the bead. The finger or knuckle behind the cloth applies controlled pressure to ensure the cloth contacts the glass rather than just the frame.

Done correctly, edge detailing takes about 10–15 seconds per pane and produces a completely clean, dry perimeter. Done incorrectly (with a wet cloth, a fibrous cloth, or insufficient pressure), it smears the water bead rather than removing it.

Most DIY window cleaning skips edge detailing or does it inadequately. The result is windows that looked clean immediately after cleaning but reveal a distinct water-line border when light hits them at an angle. This is often what homeowners are describing when they say their windows never look quite clean even after washing them.

One practical note: the cloth used for edge detailing must be lint-free. A slightly fluffy microfibre, a kitchen towel, or a chamois that's been washing with fabric softener all leave visible fibres on the glass, which defeats the purpose of the exercise.

sections.h2: How Weather Affects Squeegee Technique
sections.body:
Professional window cleaners adjust their approach based on weather conditions in ways that homeowners rarely consider. Temperature, direct sunlight, and wind all affect how cleaning solution behaves on glass and how technique must compensate.

Direct sunlight on the glass surface during cleaning is the most common weather-related cause of streaks. Sun heats the glass surface, causing the cleaning solution to dry faster than the squeegee can remove it. The dried solution leaves mineral and surfactant residue that appears as haze or streaking. Professionals either work on the shaded side of the house first, work early morning or evening when sun angle is lower, or use faster squeegee technique and smaller sections in direct sun.

Cold temperatures (below 5°C) change rubber hardness. Standard rubber becomes stiffer and loses the conformability that allows it to seal against the glass surface cleanly. In cold conditions, professionals either switch to softer-compound winter rubber or add a small amount of windshield washer fluid to the cleaning solution, which lowers the freezing point and maintains solution fluidity.

High wind creates overspray and dries the solution unevenly across the pane. In strong wind, professionals close off the area being worked (positioning themselves between the wind and the glass) and reduce the time between application and squeegee pass.

In the Shuswap region, spring and fall cleaning often means variable conditions—warm mornings, strong afternoon Chinook-effect winds, rapid temperature changes as weather moves through. Professionals working in this region account for these variables as a matter of routine.
bullets: Direct sunlight on glass causes solution to dry before the squeegee removes it—work shaded sides first
Cold below 5°C stiffens rubber—switch to softer compound or add windshield fluid to solution
High wind dries solution unevenly—minimize time between application and squeegee pass
Shuswap's variable spring/fall conditions require adaptable technique

sections.h2: Why Professional Technique Is Harder Than It Looks
sections.body:
Watching a skilled window cleaner work is disarming—it looks effortless. The T-bar goes on, the squeegee follows, the edge is detailed, and the window is done in under a minute. The ease comes from several thousand repetitions, not from the simplicity of the task.

The variables in squeegee technique compound quickly. Blade angle to glass affects both efficiency and streak production. Pressure applied across the stroke affects water removal completeness. Stroke speed affects how much water the blade manages per pass. The amount of solution applied by the T-bar affects how many passes the squeegee needs. All four of these variables interact—changing one requires compensating adjustments to the others.

Developing reliable technique on standard panes typically takes weeks of daily practice. Developing it on all pane types—large architectural windows, divided-light windows with multiple small panes, windows with surface irregularities or deep frames—takes considerably longer.

This skill development is what professional window cleaners are paid for, in addition to their equipment investment and insurance coverage. When you hire Wagon Windows, you're not paying someone to do something you could do just as well—you're paying for practised technique that produces results that are genuinely difficult to replicate without that practice.

Wagon Windows serves Salmon Arm and the Shuswap region with professional equipment and developed technique. Call (403) 837-5092 or visit wagonwindow.com to schedule service.

faqs.q: Why do my windows streak when I clean them myself?
faqs.a: Streaks come from one or more of: back-and-forth wiping technique (redepositing dirty water), worn or low-quality rubber that doesn't cut water cleanly, cleaning in direct sunlight where solution dries before squeegee removal, insufficient solution on the glass creating drag, or a fibrous cloth used for edge detailing. Fixing all four typically eliminates streaking.

faqs.q: What is the fan technique in window cleaning?
faqs.a: The fan technique is a professional squeegee method where the blade is continuously repositioned in a fan arc across the glass, keeping the squeegee in contact and moving water off the glass without lifting and repositioning for each stroke. It's efficient on medium to large panes and produces excellent results with practised technique. Beginners typically start with the straight pull method.

faqs.q: How often should squeegee rubber be replaced?
faqs.a: In professional use, rubber is flipped (using the second edge) after noticeable performance decline and replaced entirely when both edges are worn. For homeowners who clean occasionally, rubber lasts longer but should still be inspected for edge nicks or rounding. If your squeegee consistently produces chatter marks or uneven streaks even with correct technique, replace the rubber.

faqs.q: Is it better to clean windows in sun or shade?
faqs.a: Shade is strongly preferred. Direct sunlight heats the glass and causes cleaning solution to evaporate before the squeegee removes it, leaving residue. Professional cleaners work the shaded side of a home first and time their work to minimize direct-sun cleaning. If you must clean in sun, work in small sections and squeegee immediately after applying solution.

cta_text: Want streak-free results without the technique learning curve? Book Wagon Windows. Call (403) 837-5092 or visit wagonwindow.com. Residential exterior from $169.

meta_title: What Is Window Track Cleaning and Why Does It Matter?
meta_desc: Window tracks collect pollen, insects, debris, and mould—especially in the Shuswap. Learn what track cleaning involves and why neglecting it causes real damage.
h1: What Is Window Track Cleaning? Why Your Shuswap Home Needs It
intro: Window tracks are the channels that your window sashes slide in—and they are among the most neglected surfaces in the average home. Over time, they accumulate a concentrated mix of insect carcasses, pollen, cottonwood fluff, soil, and moisture that creates conditions for mould growth and mechanical failure. Window track cleaning isn't glamorous, but it's one of the most functionally important parts of a thorough window service.

sections.h2: What Window Tracks Are and Why They Accumulate Debris
sections.body:
Window tracks are the grooved channels on the interior window frame that guide the sashes as they slide open and closed. On a typical double-hung window, there are two tracks per window—one for each sash. On sliding windows, there's typically one upper and one lower track. On casement windows, the tracks or guides are less exposed but still accumulate debris around hinges and sill channels.

Tracks are positioned at the perimeter of the window opening, which makes them excellent collection points for anything that enters through or around the window. When a window is open, air carrying pollen, dust, and insects flows through the opening—and a significant fraction of that material falls into the track channel rather than passing through. Cottonwood season in the Shuswap turns this from a slow accumulation into a rapid packing problem; cottonwood fibres are light, voluminous, and find their way into every crevice.

The track design itself promotes accumulation. The channel walls and the sash rollers or slide points create corners and crevices that trap material. Debris that enters doesn't easily exit—wind doesn't blow through a track the way it passes through an open room. Material that falls in stays in unless physically removed.

Water compounds the accumulation problem significantly. Rain and condensation enter the track channel and combine with organic debris—pollen, insect remains, cottonwood fibres, soil particles—to create the ideal growth medium for mould and mildew. Tracks that are perpetually slightly damp, packed with organic material, and enclosed enough to limit air circulation will grow mould reliably. In the Shuswap, where summer humidity from the lake combines with the region's significant cottonwood and pollen season, track mould is extremely common.

sections.h2: What Professional Window Track Cleaning Actually Involves
sections.body:
Track cleaning is a distinct process from window glass cleaning—it requires different tools, more time per window, and attention to components that glass cleaning doesn't touch.

The professional track cleaning sequence begins with dry debris removal. A vacuum with a crevice attachment draws out loose material—pollen, cottonwood, insect debris, dry soil—from the full depth of the track channel. Skipping this step and going straight to wet cleaning turns dry debris into mud, which is harder to remove and can clog weep holes.

After vacuuming, a detail brush (typically a stiff-bristled brush designed for the track profile) is worked along the full length of the channel, dislodging material that the vacuum couldn't lift. Corners and the area around sash rollers are particularly debris-dense and need deliberate attention.

A cleaning solution—appropriate concentration to address mould without damaging track materials—is applied and allowed to dwell. The brush is worked through again with solution, and the resulting slurry is removed with a cloth or follow-up vacuum pass.

Weep holes—small drainage openings at the bottom of the track designed to drain water that enters the channel—are cleared individually. Blocked weep holes are one of the primary causes of water pooling in tracks, which accelerates seal damage and mould growth. A small pick tool or pipe cleaner clears packed debris from each opening.

Finally, sash rollers or slide points are lubricated with an appropriate dry lubricant (typically silicone spray, not WD-40, which attracts dust). Lubrication restores smooth sash movement and protects the roller bearing from debris-related wear.
bullets: Dry vacuum first: removes loose debris before wet cleaning creates mud
Detail brush scrub: dislodges compacted material in corners and around rollers
Solution dwell and re-scrub: addresses mould and bonded organic debris
Weep hole clearing: ensures drainage channels are open and functional
Dry lubricant application: restores smooth sash movement, protects rollers

sections.h2: Consequences of Neglected Window Tracks
sections.body:
Window track neglect produces a cascade of problems that starts as cosmetic and ends as structural. Understanding what's at stake makes the case for regular track cleaning more compelling than aesthetics alone.

The first consequence is mechanical: packed debris prevents the sash from moving smoothly. What starts as slightly stiff window operation progresses to windows that require significant force to open, and eventually to sashes that won't move at all. Forcing a window open or closed against compacted track debris puts stress on the sash frame, the hardware, and the glazing—all of which are expensive to repair or replace.

The second consequence is water damage. When weep holes are blocked by accumulated debris, water that enters the track has nowhere to drain. It pools in the channel and eventually reaches the wooden or composite frame material at the track edge. Sustained moisture contact causes wood rot, composite swelling, and the degradation of the seal between the window frame and the surrounding wall assembly. Water that gets behind the frame is expensive to remediate.

The third consequence is mould. Track mould is often the first mould in a home to develop because the conditions—organic material, moisture, limited air circulation—are persistently ideal. Track mould produces spores that are released into indoor air every time the window is operated. In bedrooms and sleeping areas, this creates a chronic low-level air quality issue that affects occupants, particularly those with allergies or respiratory sensitivities.

The fourth consequence is seal failure. Window seals—particularly the compression seals between the sash and the frame—deteriorate faster when exposed to mould, debris, and the acid conditions that accumulating organic matter creates. Seal failure means reduced thermal efficiency (noticeable energy cost) and, eventually, condensation between panes in double-glazed units.

sections.h2: Shuswap-Specific Track Challenges: Cottonwood and Lake Humidity
sections.body:
The Shuswap region creates track accumulation conditions that are notably more intense than most Canadian residential environments. Two regional factors dominate: cottonwood season and lake-driven humidity.

Cottonwood trees are widespread throughout the Shuswap valley and are prolific seeders. In late spring and early summer—typically May through June in the Salmon Arm area—cottonwood releases a heavy load of white fibrous material that collects on every exposed surface. Window tracks during cottonwood season pack densely in a matter of days. Cottonwood fibres are long, light, and interlocking—once packed, they don't dislodge with wind or light brushing. They must be physically removed.

Lake humidity from Shuswap Lake, Mara Lake, and the associated creek systems creates persistently elevated moisture conditions compared to drier inland regions. The combination of packed cottonwood fibres—highly absorbent—and elevated ambient humidity means that Shuswap-area window tracks stay damp through the summer in ways that tracks in drier climates don't. Damp organic material growing mould is essentially the predictable outcome.

Additionally, the Shuswap's mild winters and longer frost-free season extend the active insect season. Insects seeking warmth or moisture find their way into window tracks, and their remains add to the organic load that feeds mould growth.

For Shuswap homeowners, track cleaning isn't just maintenance—it's a response to a specifically challenging environment. Annual track cleaning as part of a spring service is appropriate for most homes; for lakefront properties or homes with heavy cottonwood exposure, twice-yearly cleaning may be warranted.
bullets: Cottonwood season (May–June) packs tracks densely in days, not weeks
Shuswap Lake humidity keeps tracks damp—ideal conditions for mould
Longer frost-free season extends insect activity and track debris accumulation
Lakefront and high-cottonwood-exposure homes may need twice-yearly track cleaning

sections.h2: Track Cleaning as Part of a Complete Window Service
sections.body:
Track cleaning is most efficiently done as part of a complete window service—when the screens have already been removed for glass cleaning, the windows are accessible, and the cleaning equipment is already on site.

Wagon Windows includes track cleaning as an available add-on to our residential window cleaning service, or as part of our comprehensive residential packages. When we perform track cleaning, we follow the full sequence described above: vacuum, dry brush, wet scrub with appropriate solution, weep hole clearing, and roller lubrication.

We assess each window's track condition independently. Windows with cosmetic dust accumulation get a lighter treatment; windows with significant mould presence or blocked weep holes get the full remediation process. We communicate what we're finding to the homeowner as part of the service—if we see evidence of frame moisture contact or seal deterioration, we flag it.

Track cleaning is priced separately from glass cleaning because it's a substantially different process requiring different tools and additional time per window. Ask for track cleaning to be included when you book, or discuss it as a standalone service for homes that need remediation.

Call Wagon Windows at (403) 837-5092 to discuss track cleaning as part of your next window service. Residential exterior glass cleaning starts at $169; track cleaning pricing is based on window count and condition.

faqs.q: How do I know if my window tracks need cleaning?
faqs.a: Look into the track channel with a flashlight. If you see visible debris accumulation—cottonwood fibres, insect remains, dark discolouration from mould, soil compaction—cleaning is overdue. Functionally: if your windows are stiff to operate, if water pools in the sill area after rain, or if you notice a musty smell when windows are open, these all indicate track problems.

faqs.q: Can I clean window tracks myself?
faqs.a: Yes, for light maintenance. A vacuum with a crevice tool, a stiff brush, and a cleaning solution appropriate for mould (dilute bleach or white vinegar) handles basic track cleaning. The step most homeowners miss is clearing weep holes—use a toothpick or small pick to clear each hole individually. For significant mould accumulation or blocked drainage causing water damage, professional cleaning is more thorough.

faqs.q: What is a weep hole in a window track, and why does it matter?
faqs.a: Weep holes are small drainage openings at the bottom of window track channels, designed to drain water that enters the track from rain, condensation, or window operation. When weep holes are blocked by debris, water pools in the channel, leading to frame moisture damage, seal degradation, and mould growth. Clearing weep holes is one of the most functional steps in a thorough track cleaning.

faqs.q: Does Wagon Windows offer track cleaning in Salmon Arm?
faqs.a: Yes. Track cleaning is available as an add-on to our residential window cleaning service or as a standalone service for homes that need track remediation. We cover Salmon Arm, Sorrento, Tappen, Armstrong, and the broader Shuswap region. Call (403) 837-5092 to discuss what your windows need.

cta_text: Cottonwood season is hard on Shuswap window tracks. Book Wagon Windows for complete track cleaning alongside your window service. Call (403) 837-5092.

meta_title: Window Screen Cleaning Guide for Shuswap Homeowners
meta_desc: When to clean window screens, DIY vs professional, and why cottonwood pollen and lake humidity make screens a bigger issue in Salmon Arm than most regions.
h1: Window Screen Cleaning Guide: What Shuswap Homeowners Need to Know
intro: Window screens do their job invisibly when they're clean—and visibly when they're not. A dirty screen reduces airflow, blocks natural light, retains moisture that feeds mould, and passes a fine layer of pollen and particulate into your home every time a breeze moves through. In the Shuswap, where cottonwood season and lake humidity create aggressive screen-fouling conditions, screen cleaning deserves more attention than most homeowners give it.

sections.h2: When Do Window Screens Actually Need Cleaning?
sections.body:
Most homeowners clean their screens when they visually appear dirty—when they can see accumulated debris. That's a reasonable baseline, but waiting for visible fouling means the screen has already progressed past the point of affecting air quality and airflow.

Several conditions indicate that cleaning is due regardless of visual appearance. If allergy symptoms worsen when windows are open versus closed, screen contamination is a likely contributor—even a fine, visually subtle coating of pollen on screen mesh significantly increases the pollen load carried indoors by breezes. If you notice reduced airflow through open windows compared to when screens are removed, the screen mesh is clogged enough to restrict air movement.

Visual indicators worth acting on: any visible pollen coating (yellow or grey-green haze on the mesh), visible mildew darkening at frame corners, cottonwood fibres embedded in the mesh that persist after light brushing, or any fuzzy growth on the aluminum frame. Post-winter appearance should always be assessed—screens that spent winter in place typically carry moisture-driven growth that isn't obvious until you look closely.

For Shuswap-area homes, the practical answer is twice-yearly cleaning as a baseline: once in late spring after cottonwood season, once in fall before screens are removed or covered for winter. Lakefront properties and homes with significant tree cover—cottonwood, poplar, or high-pollen ornamental species—benefit from more frequent attention.

For homes where screens stay in place year-round, annual cleaning at minimum prevents the gradual accumulation that reduces screen performance and shortens frame life.
bullets: Allergy symptoms worsen with windows open: screen pollen loading likely
Reduced airflow through open windows: mesh clogged enough to restrict passage
Visible pollen, mildew, or cottonwood fibres embedded in mesh
Post-winter assessment: moisture-driven growth typically present on year-round screens

sections.h2: DIY Screen Cleaning: What Works and What Doesn't
sections.body:
DIY screen cleaning is genuinely manageable for most homeowners with the right approach. The tools required are simple, and the technique is forgiving compared to glass cleaning. The common mistakes are in force and drying.

The effective DIY screen cleaning process: remove each screen and lay it flat on a clean surface (or lean it against a fence). Use a soft brush—a natural-bristle paintbrush or a dedicated screen cleaning brush—to dry-brush loose debris off both faces of the mesh before introducing water. This step removes cottonwood fibres and loose pollen before it can become muddy paste when wet.

After dry brushing, rinse the screen with a garden hose on a gentle setting—not a pressure washer or strong jet stream, which can distort the mesh or damage spline seals. Work with the flow of water, not against it, and cover both faces. For screens with mildew growth, a dilute solution of dish soap or mild cleaner can be applied with the soft brush after the initial rinse.

After washing, shake excess water and prop the screen to drain and air-dry completely before reinstalling. Reinstalling damp screens introduces moisture to the track and frame, contributing to the mould conditions you were trying to address.

The mistake most homeowners make is using a pressure washer at too-high a setting, which bows aluminum frames and distorts fine mesh. The second common mistake is reinstalling screens while still damp—this traps moisture behind the spline seal and promotes mould at the frame corners.

For screens with significant mildew, embedded cottonwood, or visible frame corrosion, DIY cleaning may produce limited results. Professional cleaning using appropriate solutions and proper rinsing technique is more thorough.

sections.h2: Professional Screen Cleaning vs. DIY: Where the Gap Is
sections.body:
Professional screen cleaning produces better results in specific situations—not because the task is inherently complex, but because professionals use appropriate solutions, have the equipment to handle volume efficiently, and apply technique developed through repetition.

For standard dusty or lightly-pollen-coated screens, thorough DIY cleaning produces results very close to professional quality. The difference is small for simple cases.

The gap widens for: screens with embedded cottonwood fibres in fine mesh (professional brushing technique and solution dwell time removes more material), screens with significant mildew growth (professional cleaners use mould-killing solutions and technique that ensures full treatment of frame corners), and screens with visible corrosion or staining on aluminum frames (professional cleaning can address these without using abrasives that damage mesh).

A practical advantage of professional screen cleaning is systematic organization. When a home has 15–20 screens removed, washed, and ready for reinstall, the risk of putting the wrong screen in the wrong window is significant. Wrong-size screens either don't seat properly or are forced into place, distorting the frame. Professional cleaners mark or organize screens by window position before removal and reinstall each screen in its correct location.

Cottonwood in the Shuswap is a specific professional advantage case. Cottonwood fibres are not easily removed by hose rinsing—they embed in mesh and interlock with neighbouring fibres. A stiff brush with appropriate solution, applied with enough contact force to dislodge the fibres without damaging mesh, removes more material than most homeowners achieve with a garden hose.
bullets: DIY works well for standard dust and light pollen accumulation
Professional cleaning outperforms DIY on embedded cottonwood fibres
Mildew treatment requires appropriate solutions and complete coverage of frame corners
Screen organization and correct reinstallation is a professional process advantage

sections.h2: Shuswap-Specific Challenges: Cottonwood and Lake Humidity
sections.body:
The Shuswap region presents screen-fouling conditions that are more severe than most Canadian communities face. Two regional factors dominate.

Cottonwood pollen season in the Salmon Arm area typically runs from late May through June, with intensity varying by year. Cottonwood releases voluminous fibrous material that is both visible and persistent. Unlike tree pollen, which is a fine powder that rinses away easily, cottonwood fibres are structural—they interlock with the mesh structure of fine-gauge screens and resist rinsing. A week of open windows during peak cottonwood can pack fine-mesh screens to the point of near-complete air blockage. The problem is worse with newer, finer-mesh screens (such as 20-mesh or finer insect screens) than with standard 18x16 fiberglass mesh.

Lake humidity from Shuswap Lake and associated waterways creates persistently elevated moisture conditions throughout the summer. Screens that hold moisture—especially at frame corners where the spline seal meets aluminum—develop mould reliably. The mould colony at a screen corner isn't typically visible from the ground; it's discovered when the screen is removed for cleaning. But it produces spores that enter the home through the screen mesh whenever the window is open and a breeze flows through.

Aluminum frames are subject to corrosion in high-humidity environments, particularly near the lake. Fine white corrosion deposits on aluminum frame surfaces indicate oxidation—cleaning can remove surface deposits, but significant corrosion signals a screen that should be replaced rather than repeatedly cleaned.

For Shuswap homeowners, the practical recommendation is: remove and inspect all screens in late spring (after cottonwood), clean thoroughly, inspect frames for corrosion, and reinstall only screens in good condition. Screens showing significant frame corrosion or mesh damage should be replaced.

sections.h2: Screen Inspection: What to Check During Cleaning
sections.body:
Screen cleaning is the natural opportunity to inspect each screen's condition. Assessments made during cleaning inform replacement decisions and prevent damaged screens from being reinstalled where they won't perform their function.

Mesh integrity: Look for tears, holes, or stretched sections. Even small holes defeat the insect-exclusion function of the screen. Pets are a common cause of mesh damage at lower windows; cottonwood debris and pressure washing can also cause damage over time.

Frame condition: Bent or bowed aluminum frames don't seal against the window opening properly. A minor bow can be pressed back into shape; significant deformation means the screen should be replaced. Corrosion—visible as white powder deposits on aluminum—is cosmetic at early stages and structural at advanced stages.

Spline condition: The rubber spline holds the mesh in the frame channel. Old or dried spline can pop out of the channel when the screen is handled, releasing the mesh. Spline replacement is a DIY repair (the material costs a few dollars and the tool costs about $10); it's worthwhile if the screen frame itself is in good condition.

Rubber seal condition: The foam or rubber sealing strip at the screen perimeter—where the screen contacts the window frame—should be intact and compressible. Deteriorated seals allow insects to enter around the screen perimeter.

Wagon Windows inspects each screen during removal and cleaning and communicates findings to the homeowner. If a screen needs spline replacement or has mesh damage, we note it. Replacement recommendations are based on condition, not routine replacement pressure—if a screen is in good shape, we say so.
bullets: Mesh: check for tears, holes, stretch marks from pets or pressure washing
Frame: check for bowing, corrosion, or deformation that prevents proper sealing
Spline: confirm it seats fully in the channel and isn't cracked or hardened
Edge seal: confirm compressible foam/rubber seal is intact around perimeter

faqs.q: How often should window screens be cleaned in the Shuswap?
faqs.a: Twice yearly is appropriate for most Shuswap-area homes: once in late spring after cottonwood season, once in fall. Lakefront properties and homes with heavy cottonwood tree coverage may benefit from additional attention during peak cottonwood in May–June. Screens that stay in place year-round should be inspected and cleaned at least annually.

faqs.q: Will a garden hose remove cottonwood from window screens?
faqs.a: Partially. A garden hose on a gentle setting removes loose cottonwood before it embeds deeply. Once cottonwood fibres have interlocked with the mesh structure, rinsing alone is insufficient. A soft brush with cleaning solution, worked carefully into the mesh from both sides, removes more embedded material than rinsing alone. Avoid high-pressure settings, which distort mesh and damage frames.

faqs.q: Can mould on window screen frames affect indoor air quality?
faqs.a: Yes. Mould colonies on screen frames—particularly at corners where the spline meets the frame—produce spores that are carried indoors on air currents whenever the window is open. For occupants with mould allergies or respiratory sensitivities, this can produce noticeable symptoms. Cleaning with an appropriate mould-killing solution and ensuring screens dry completely before reinstallation addresses the issue.

faqs.q: Does Wagon Windows clean and reinstall window screens?
faqs.a: Yes. Screen cleaning is available as part of our residential window service. We remove, clean, inspect, and reinstall each screen in its correct window position. Screens are organized by location before removal to prevent the common problem of reinstalling a screen in the wrong window. Ask about screen cleaning when you book—call (403) 837-5092.

cta_text: Cottonwood season takes a toll on Shuswap window screens. Book Wagon Windows for screen cleaning as part of your spring service. Call (403) 837-5092.

meta_title: Exterior vs Interior Window Cleaning: What's Included
meta_desc: What does exterior window cleaning include vs interior? Understand the difference in scope, tools, and when exterior-only makes sense for your home.
h1: Exterior vs Interior Window Cleaning: What Each Service Includes and When You Need Both
intro: Exterior window cleaning and full interior-exterior window cleaning sound like simple variations on the same service—but they're distinct processes requiring different tools, different access logistics, and different time investments. Understanding what each service includes helps you choose the right option for your home, your schedule, and your budget.

sections.h2: What Exterior Window Cleaning Includes
sections.body:
Exterior window cleaning addresses the outside face of every accessible window pane on the home. It's the more commonly booked service for a simple reason: the exterior faces are by far the dirtier surfaces. Weather, dust, pollen, bird activity, spider webs, and—in the Shuswap—mineral deposits from lake spray and irrigation all accumulate on exterior glass. Interior surfaces, by contrast, accumulate primarily household dust, occasional fingerprints, and condensation residue.

A thorough exterior cleaning includes: removal of any loose debris, cobwebs, and dry contaminants from the glass surface before solution is applied (dry brushing); application of professional cleaning solution via T-bar applicator; squeegee removal with the single-pass technique; edge detailing with a lint-free cloth; and a final wipe of the exterior windowsill and frame surround.

Screen removal before exterior cleaning and reinstallation after is standard—screens left in place block direct access to the glass and produce inferior results. When screens are removed as part of the service, they're typically shaken or brushed to remove loose debris before reinstallation. Full screen cleaning is a separate service from this light removal-and-reinstall step.

Exterior cleaning also addresses frame condition in a functional sense: the squeegee and sill wipe process removes surface debris from frames, though it doesn't include deep track cleaning (a separate service) or pressure washing of frames.

For most residential properties, exterior cleaning is the most impactful single service. The visual difference is dramatic—dirty exterior glass makes the whole home look neglected; clean exterior glass has an immediate curb appeal effect that interior cleaning alone cannot achieve.

sections.h2: What Interior Window Cleaning Adds
sections.body:
Interior window cleaning addresses the inside face of each window pane. When booked as a full service alongside exterior cleaning, the cleaner works both faces of each window in sequence—typically exterior first, then interior, so any light exterior residue doesn't contaminate the interior glass on the return pass.

The interior process is similar in technique—T-bar applicator, squeegee, edge detailing—but differs in access logistics and tool management. Interior cleaning requires moving furniture or objects near windows, working in occupied living spaces, and managing water more carefully to prevent floor or surface contact. Professional cleaners work with cloths to catch drips immediately and take care around soft furnishings, electronics, and any surfaces near the window.

Interior cleaning also addresses accumulated condensation residue and film that forms on interior glass over time. In kitchens and bathrooms, cooking vapour and steam contribute to a greasy film that doesn't exist on exterior glass. In living areas, dust mixed with outgassing from furnishings creates a subtle haze that's most visible in oblique light. These interior-specific deposits require the same squeegee technique as exterior cleaning but may need a slightly more concentrated solution or a second application for heavier films.

Interior cleaning typically adds 30–60 minutes to a full-home service, depending on window count and access complexity. It's priced in addition to exterior cleaning—ask about the combined price when booking.
bullets: Interior service adds access logistics: furniture movement, floor protection, drip management
Kitchen and bathroom interior glass develops grease and steam film not present on exteriors
Living area glass develops dust-and-outgassing haze visible in oblique light
Adds 30–60 minutes to a full-home service; priced in addition to exterior

sections.h2: When Exterior-Only Makes Sense
sections.body:
For many homeowners and many scheduling situations, exterior-only cleaning is the right choice—not a compromise, but a deliberate and sensible selection.

Exterior-only is appropriate when: interior windows are reasonably clean (regular household maintenance keeps interior glass in acceptable condition); the primary motivation is curb appeal or pre-event preparation; scheduling requires minimal home access or occupation time; or budget requires prioritizing the highest-impact service.

Because exterior glass accumulates far more contamination than interior glass, exterior cleaning produces proportionally more visual impact per dollar spent. A home with clean exterior glass and slightly dusty interior glass looks significantly cleaner than the reverse—the exterior face is what catches light from outside and what guests and passersby see.

Exterior-only is also appropriate for homeowners who prefer to clean interior windows themselves or who have pets or small children making coordinating interior access logistically complex. The service can be completed efficiently with no one home, while interior cleaning typically benefits from the homeowner being present to move objects near windows.

For pre-sale preparation, the decision depends on buyer demographic and showing conditions. In strong seller's markets, exterior-only usually covers the visual requirement. In competitive markets where buyers scrutinize thoroughly, full interior-exterior cleaning ensures no detail reads as neglected.

Wagon Windows offers exterior-only and full interior-exterior packages. We'll recommend what makes sense for your situation when you call—not upsell for the sake of it.

sections.h2: How the Two Services Differ in Execution
sections.body:
Beyond scope, exterior and interior window cleaning differ in the tools used, the cleaning solutions appropriate, and the care required during execution.

Exterior cleaning is physically active work—cleaners move around the building, use extension poles and ladders for upper floors, manage buckets and equipment on outdoor surfaces, and work in weather conditions (wind, direct sun, temperature variations). The work is efficient because access is relatively uncomplicated and drips and water contact with exterior surfaces are generally not a concern.

Interior cleaning requires more careful management. Solution application must account for walls, floors, and furnishings within splatter range. Squeegee pulls must be controlled to prevent water from running off the sill onto interior surfaces. Drop cloths or towels are positioned at the sill line to catch any water. Cleaners working indoors are also more careful about footwear, equipment placement, and communication with the homeowner about what needs to be moved.

The cleaning solution used may differ slightly. Interior glass that carries cooking film or condensation residue may benefit from a slightly higher surfactant concentration than standard exterior cleaning solution. Ammonia-based solutions are sometimes used for interior kitchen glass where grease film is present—but not on tinted or coated glass, where ammonia can cause discolouration.

Professionally, the two services are often performed by the same cleaner on the same visit, moving systematically through the home. For large homes with many windows, some companies use two-person teams—one cleaner working exterior, one interior—to reduce total visit time.

sections.h2: Which Service Is More Popular and Why
sections.body:
In the Shuswap residential market, exterior-only cleaning is the more commonly booked service, and the reasons are practical.

First, exterior glass is the dirtier surface by a significant margin. Shuswap-area homes face weather exposure, pollen, cottonwood, and mineral deposits from lake and irrigation water. Exterior glass can go from professionally clean to visibly dirty in a single week during peak cottonwood or following a rain event that leaves mineral deposits. Interior glass, by comparison, accumulates slowly.

Second, exterior cleaning requires less homeowner coordination. There's no need to be home, no need to move furniture, and no concern about cleaners in occupied living spaces. For busy homeowners or properties that are seasonally occupied (many Shuswap lake properties are), the ability to book exterior cleaning without being present is a meaningful convenience.

Third, exterior cleaning produces the visible impact that motivates the decision to book in the first place. Homeowners notice dirty exterior glass from the driveway. The improvement from a professional exterior clean is immediately visible and satisfying in a way that interior cleaning—which addresses subtler accumulation—is not.

Full interior-exterior cleaning is more common for pre-sale preparation, post-construction cleaning, spring deep-cleaning projects, and clients who want comprehensive service on a seasonal schedule. These homeowners value the complete result over the convenience of exterior-only access.

Wagon Windows books both services regularly across Salmon Arm and the Shuswap. Call (403) 837-5092 to discuss what's right for your home and timeline. Residential exterior starts at $169.

faqs.q: What's the difference between exterior and interior window cleaning?
faqs.a: Exterior window cleaning addresses the outside face of every accessible pane—the dirtiest surface, exposed to weather, pollen, and mineral deposits. Interior cleaning addresses the inside face, which accumulates household dust, condensation residue, and cooking film more slowly. Full service covers both; exterior-only is the more commonly booked option and produces most of the visible impact.

faqs.q: Do I need to be home for exterior window cleaning?
faqs.a: Generally no. Exterior cleaning is conducted entirely outside the home and doesn't require interior access. Cleaners work around the building perimeter and can complete the service without the homeowner present. Interior cleaning does benefit from the homeowner being home to facilitate object movement near windows, though this can be arranged in advance.

faqs.q: How much more does interior window cleaning cost compared to exterior-only?
faqs.a: Interior cleaning adds 30–50% to a typical residential exterior cleaning price, depending on home size and access complexity. The additional cost reflects the added time, care, and logistics of working indoors. Wagon Windows prices the combined service transparently—call (403) 837-5092 for a quote that covers both.

faqs.q: Does Wagon Windows clean both interior and exterior windows?
faqs.a: Yes. We offer exterior-only and full interior-exterior residential cleaning. Exterior cleaning starts at $169. Full interior-exterior cleaning is quoted based on home size and window count. We recommend the right service for your situation—we don't push interior if exterior alone serves your needs.

cta_text: Book exterior or full-home window cleaning with Wagon Windows. Serving Salmon Arm and the Shuswap. Call (403) 837-5092. Residential exterior from $169.

meta_title: Professional Window Cleaning Process: Step by Step
meta_desc: See exactly how Wagon Windows cleans your home's windows—from walkthrough to final inspection. Know what a thorough job looks like and what corners cheap services cut.
h1: The Professional Window Cleaning Process: A Step-by-Step Guide to How Wagon Windows Works
intro: Professional window cleaning looks simple from the outside—a cleaner shows up, does their work, and leaves behind spotless glass. What's less visible is the systematic process that makes that outcome consistent. This is the full Wagon Windows process, step by step—every stage, why it matters, and what separates thorough service from the shortcuts that cheaper operators take.

sections.h2: Step 1: Walkthrough and Pre-Service Assessment
sections.body:
Before any cleaning begins, Wagon Windows conducts a walkthrough of the property with the homeowner or, on unoccupied properties, a systematic independent assessment. This step is non-negotiable and sets the entire service up for success.

The walkthrough accomplishes several things. First, it identifies access challenges: is the area under each ladder placement stable? Are there irrigation heads, decorative stones, or plantings that require care during approach? Are there second-storey windows with unusual setbacks or architectural features?

Second, it identifies special conditions: visible mineral deposits requiring treatment? Post-construction residue? Windows with specialty coatings that preclude standard chemical protocols? Any broken seals in double-pane units that will fog after cleaning (addressing homeowner expectation: a broken seal fogs regardless of how the glass is cleaned—this isn't a cleaning failure)?

Third, it confirms scope: which windows are included in the service (all accessible panes, or specific inclusions/exclusions)? Are screens to be cleaned as part of the service or just removed and reinstalled? Are tracks included?

Fourth, it notes any pre-existing glass damage that the homeowner should be aware of before cleaning begins—chips, cracks, delaminating film—so that there's no ambiguity about when damage occurred.

This walkthrough is documented. Anything noteworthy is noted in the service agreement. Homeowners are informed of any findings that affect scope, pricing, or expected outcome before work begins. This transparency protects both parties and ensures expectations are aligned.
bullets: Access assessment: ladder placement stability, obstacles, architectural complexities
Special condition identification: mineral deposits, post-construction residue, coated glass
Scope confirmation: which windows, screens, tracks, and services are included
Pre-existing damage notation: chips, cracks, broken seals noted before cleaning begins

sections.h2: Step 2: Screen Removal and Organization
sections.body:
Once the walkthrough is complete, screens are removed systematically. This sounds simple—it isn't, at scale.

A home with 20 windows has 20 screens, each of which fits one specific window. Screens that appear similar in size often have subtle differences—a millimetre difference in height or width, a screen with a different mesh gauge installed in a particular window, or a screen with a repair that the homeowner knows about and wants kept in a specific location. Installing the wrong screen in the wrong window means either a screen that doesn't seat properly (creating an insect-entry gap) or a screen forced into place (bowing the frame).

Wagon Windows removes screens in a documented sequence, marking or organizing them by window position before moving on. The organization system ensures every screen goes back to its original location. This seems like overhead—it is, but it prevents a problem that generates complaints in operations that don't do it.

During removal, screens are inspected for damage: mesh tears, spline condition, frame bowing, corrosion. Any findings are communicated to the homeowner. Screens with significant damage are flagged for replacement discussion rather than silently reinstalled.

Removed screens are placed in a safe, organized location away from the working area—not leaning against the wall where a ladder could knock them, not in the path of water runoff from cleaning.

sections.h2: Step 3: Dry Debris Removal and Pre-Rinse
sections.body:
Before any cleaning solution touches the glass, dry debris is removed. This step is skipped by under-equipped operators, and its absence is felt in the result.

Dry debris on exterior glass includes: spider webs and insect carcasses at frame corners, loose cottonwood fibres, pollen dust, bird droppings that haven't been moistened, and any dry soil from splash-back at lower panes. When cleaning solution is applied to glass that hasn't been dry-cleared, this debris becomes wet debris—it smears across the glass during the T-bar application stroke, redistributing rather than removing it.

A soft brush (a natural-bristle brush or professional glass-detailing brush) clears webs and insect material from frame corners and the glass surface. A dry microfibre pass removes loose pollen and dust. On panes with significant cottonwood accumulation, a more deliberate dry brush pass removes as much fibre as possible before moisture is introduced.

Bird droppings deserve specific attention. Fresh or recently-moistened droppings are acidic—they actively etch glass if allowed to dwell and are also corrosive to certain frame coatings. Dried droppings require pre-softening with water before they can be removed without abrasion risk. The pre-rinse step—a light water mist or wet cloth applied to dried droppings—allows them to soften while the dry brush work continues on other windows.

For homes with significant organic debris (under trees, near bird nesting areas), the dry-debris step takes proportionally longer than on a cleaner home. It's time well spent: rushing past it produces inferior glass results.

sections.h2: Steps 4–6: Solution Application, Squeegee, and Edge Detailing
sections.body:
With dry debris removed and any softening done, the active cleaning sequence begins. This three-step sequence—solution, squeegee, detail—is repeated on every pane.

Solution application via T-bar: the sleeve, saturated with cleaning solution at the appropriate concentration for local water hardness, is applied to the glass in full-coverage strokes. The solution is spread evenly and allowed a brief dwell—typically 15–30 seconds on standard glass, longer on panes with mineral deposit concerns. During dwell time, the solution breaks down surface tension, penetrates mineral deposits, and loosens organic debris.

Squeegeeing: the rubber squeegee is pulled across the glass using the single-pass technique appropriate for the pane size and orientation. For small or divided-light windows, straight horizontal pulls are most controlled. For large architectural windows, the fan technique or straight vertical pulls are used depending on orientation. Each stroke overlaps the previous by approximately half an inch to capture any water that crept past the blade edge. The blade is checked and flipped if any chattering or inconsistency in the pull is noticed.

Edge detailing: after each pane is squeegeed, a lint-free cloth is used to remove the water bead at the frame perimeter. The cloth is pressed to the edge with a knuckle or folded finger, drawing down and across each side of the frame. The detailing cloth is refolded to a clean section after every two or three panes to ensure a dry surface is always in contact with the glass. A wet detailing cloth smears rather than absorbs—it's one of the most common causes of frame-edge residue that homeowners notice in oblique light.
bullets: Solution at calibrated concentration for Shuswap water hardness—never standard dilution without water assessment
Dwell time extended on mineral deposit situations—not rushed to the next step
Squeegee rubber checked and flipped if chatter develops—blade quality is maintained during the job
Detailing cloth refolded to dry section every 2–3 panes—wet cloth smears, dry cloth absorbs

sections.h2: Steps 7–10: Interior Cleaning, Screen Reinstall, and Final Inspection
sections.body:
For full interior-exterior services, the interior cleaning follows the exterior sequence using the same T-bar, squeegee, and detailing approach—with the additional care required for working in occupied living spaces. Drop cloths at sill lines, immediate drip management, and communication about anything moved near windows are standard interior protocol.

Screen reinstallation follows interior cleaning. Screens are returned to their documented positions, seated fully in the track channel, and tested for proper engagement—pressed at corners to confirm the frame seats without bowing or gap. Any screen that doesn't seat properly is flagged immediately rather than forced.

The final inspection is a walk-around assessment of every cleaned window. Each pane is viewed from multiple angles in available light—including oblique viewing that reveals any residual haze, streaking, or edge water lines not visible from straight-on. Any pane that doesn't meet standard is re-cleaned on the spot.

Photographic documentation is taken of any pre-existing damage noted during the initial walkthrough, and of the completed work where appropriate. This documentation protects both the cleaner (clear record of pre-existing conditions) and the homeowner (confirmation of completion and condition).

The service agreement records what was completed, any findings communicated during the job, and any follow-up recommendations (screens needing replacement, tracks needing separate service, broken seals that should be addressed). This paper trail means there's no ambiguity about what was done and what was communicated.

Wagon Windows doesn't leave a job until the inspection is complete and the homeowner (where present) is satisfied. Call (403) 837-5092 or visit wagonwindow.com to schedule. Residential exterior from $169.

faqs.q: How long does professional window cleaning take for a typical home?
faqs.a: A standard 2,000–2,500 sq ft Salmon Arm home with 20–25 windows takes a Wagon Windows crew approximately two to three hours for exterior-only service. Full interior-exterior cleaning adds 30–60 minutes depending on access complexity and number of windows. Homes with significant mineral deposit treatment or track cleaning included take longer.

faqs.q: What happens if a window is missed or doesn't meet quality standards?
faqs.a: Wagon Windows conducts a post-cleaning inspection before leaving the property. Any pane that doesn't meet standard is re-cleaned immediately. If you identify an issue after we leave, call us—we'll return and correct it. Service quality is our reputation, and we don't consider a job complete unless the result meets the standard we'd accept on our own home.

faqs.q: Do I need to be home during the window cleaning service?
faqs.a: For exterior-only cleaning, no—the service is conducted entirely outside and can be completed while you're away. For interior cleaning, being home to open windows and identify any objects near windows that need moving is helpful, though we can work around most situations with advance communication. Leaving a gate unlocked and confirming payment method before departure is sufficient for most exterior-only bookings.

faqs.q: What does Wagon Windows do if they find a broken window seal during cleaning?
faqs.a: We note it during the initial walkthrough or during cleaning and communicate it to the homeowner. A broken seal in a double-pane unit causes fogging between the panes regardless of how the glass is cleaned—it's not a cleaning failure, and we're transparent about that distinction. We don't clean inside a broken seal. We'll recommend the homeowner contact a window supplier about replacement, and we document the finding in the service record.

cta_text: Book a professional window clean you can trust, start to finish. Wagon Windows serves Salmon Arm and the Shuswap. Call (403) 837-5092. Residential exterior from $169.

meta_title: What to Expect From a Gutter Cleaning Service
meta_desc: Learn exactly what a professional gutter cleaning covers — debris removal, downspout flushing, condition reports — and what separates a thorough job from a rushed one.
h1: What to Expect When You Book a Professional Gutter Cleaning
intro: Booking a gutter cleaning for the first time — or switching to a new company — leaves most homeowners wondering what they're actually paying for. A professional gutter cleaning is more than scooping out leaves; done right, it includes a systematic inspection, a downspout flush, and a condition report that tells you what's coming before it becomes expensive. Here's exactly what to expect when Wagon Windows shows up at your door.

sections.h2: Step 1: Safety Setup and Site Assessment
sections.body:
Before a single piece of debris is touched, a professional crew assesses the site. That means checking the pitch and height of the roofline, identifying fragile landscaping or air conditioning units below the gutters, and confirming the ladder placement is stable on the ground surface — not just convenient.

At Wagon Windows, every job begins with a walk around the perimeter. We note overflow staining on fascia boards, any visible sags in the gutter run, and downspout discharge points before we ever set up a ladder. This five-minute walkthrough prevents the two most common mistakes in gutter cleaning: missing a section entirely and damaging plantings below the drip line.

For two-storey or steep-pitch homes, extension ladders are set at the correct angle and secured to prevent slipping. Safety isn't a formality — a ladder incident on your property without proper insurance coverage is a nightmare for both parties. Ask any company you hire whether they carry WorkSafe BC coverage before they step foot on your roof line.
bullets: Perimeter walkthrough to note visible problems before work begins
Ladder placement on stable, level ground — not gravel or soft turf without boards
Identification of downspout discharge points to verify flush path
Note of fascia staining, sags, or prior overflow damage

sections.h2: Step 2: Debris Removal — What a Thorough Job Looks Like
sections.body:
Debris removal is the core of the job, but there's a significant difference between a thorough removal and a rushed one. A proper clean removes all organic material from the full gutter run: leaves, pine needles, seed pods (cottonwood is prolific in the Shuswap), compacted sludge at the bottom of the channel, and the fine grit that accumulates near downspout entrances.

A rushed job scoops the obvious top layer and calls it done. The compacted layer of decomposing material at the bottom of the gutter trough is often ignored — and that layer is what holds moisture, accelerates corrosion in aluminum gutters, and grows the moss and algae that eventually blocks downspout screens.

At Wagon Windows, we hand-scoop debris into bags that leave your property clean — not dumped into your garden beds. This matters particularly for Salmon Arm properties with landscaped borders adjacent to the house. Debris is removed, not redistributed.

For homes with valley gutters (common on larger Shuswap lakefront homes), the valley sections require extra attention — they collect the most material and are frequently the first to fail under a heavy rain if missed.
bullets: Full channel cleared to the gutter floor — not just the top layer
Downspout entrance screens cleared of packed material
Valley sections and inside corners cleared individually
Debris bagged and removed from property

sections.h2: Step 3: The Downspout Flush — The Most Important Step
sections.body:
Many homeowners don't realize that clean-looking gutters can still have a completely blocked downspout. A downspout can be packed with compacted debris from the inside — often pine needles that have compressed over seasons — with zero visible indication from above.

The downspout flush is where a lot of budget operators cut corners. It takes time, it requires a hose or water source, and on a tall home it means going up and down the ladder twice per downspout run. But it's the single most important diagnostic step in the entire process.

A proper flush pushes water from the gutter into the downspout and confirms it exits cleanly at grade. If water backs up or flows slowly, the downspout is partially blocked. If water doesn't move at all, there's a hard blockage — typically a compressed debris plug or, in older homes, a separated elbow joint that's become a collection point.

When we identify a blocked downspout, we snake it clear and re-flush before signing off. A gutter job without a downspout confirmation is like a plumber replacing your tap without checking the water pressure. The flush is the proof.
sections.body2: In the Shuswap, downspouts that discharge onto gravel splash pads or into garden drainage channels need to be checked that the exit point isn't blocked by seasonal sediment buildup — particularly after spring runoff. We check discharge conditions as part of every flush.

sections.h2: Step 4: Fascia and Gutter Condition Inspection
sections.body:
Once the gutters are clear and flushed, a good service includes a visual inspection of the gutter system itself — not just the debris inside it. This covers the fascia boards behind the gutters (rot here means the gutters will eventually pull away from the house), the gutter hangers and spike spacing (loose spikes cause the sagging that creates standing water), and the sealant at end caps and inside corners.

This is where the difference between a premium service and a commodity one shows most clearly. Wagon Windows provides a condition report at the end of every job: a plain-language summary of anything we observed that the homeowner should know about. This might include a section of fascia showing early rot, a downspout elbow that's cracked and leaking, or a gutter run that's pulling away from the roofline and will need re-spiking before winter.

We don't upsell repairs — we note what we see and let you decide what to do about it. That transparency is what builds the kind of long-term relationship where clients call us every spring without shopping around.
bullets: Fascia board condition along the full gutter run
Hanger and spike integrity — any sections with visible sag
End cap and inside corner sealant condition
Downspout bracket attachment at wall
Discharge splash pad or underground drain connection condition

sections.h2: What a Bad Gutter Cleaning Skips (And Why It Costs You More Later)
sections.body:
The lowest-price gutter cleaning quote almost always cuts corners in a predictable sequence. First to go: the downspout flush (takes time, requires water access). Second: debris removal from the trough floor (the sludge layer). Third: the condition report (creates accountability). Fourth: the fascia check (requires actually looking, not just clearing and leaving).

The result is gutters that look clean but still overflow in the next heavy rain — because the downspout was never confirmed clear, or because the compacted bottom layer restricts flow to the downspout entrance. You've paid for a service and still have the underlying problem.

The cost difference between a thorough clean and a rushed one is rarely more than $30-50 on a typical Salmon Arm property. The cost of one water ingress event into a finished basement, or a section of rotted fascia from standing water, is measured in thousands. The math isn't complicated.

Wagon Windows serves Salmon Arm and all Shuswap communities. Call (403) 837-5092 to book or get a quote.
bullets: No downspout flush = you don't know if water actually exits the system
No trough floor clean = debris restricts flow within one rain season
No condition report = you learn about fascia rot when the gutter falls off
No fascia check = structural damage continues invisibly until it's expensive

faqs.q: How long does a professional gutter cleaning take?
faqs.a: For a standard single-storey Salmon Arm home, expect 45–90 minutes. A two-storey home with a complex roofline or heavy debris load typically runs 1.5–2.5 hours. Homes with significant cottonwood seed or pine needle accumulation take longer — the compacted material in the trough floor requires more time than loose leaves.

faqs.q: Do I need to be home when gutters are cleaned?
faqs.a: No — but we recommend it for the condition report handoff. If you can't be present, we'll photograph any items of concern and include notes with the invoice. Access to an outdoor tap for the downspout flush is the only operational requirement. Let us know your preference when booking.

faqs.q: What's included in the condition report?
faqs.a: Our condition report covers: fascia board integrity, gutter hanger and spike condition, any visible sags or separations in the gutter run, downspout flush results (clear vs. cleared vs. partially restricted), and discharge point condition at grade. It's delivered as a plain-language summary — no jargon, no pressure to book repairs.

faqs.q: How often should gutters be cleaned in Salmon Arm?
faqs.a: Twice a year is the standard recommendation for most Salmon Arm properties: once in late spring after cottonwood pollen season (June), and once in late fall after deciduous leaf drop (October–November). Homes under heavy pine or fir tree cover may need three cleans — add a late-summer clean in August to stay ahead of needle accumulation.

cta_text: Book a professional gutter cleaning with a full downspout flush and condition report. Call Wagon Windows at (403) 837-5092 or visit wagonwindow.com.

meta_title: Eco-Friendly Window Cleaning Products BC | Wagon Windows
meta_desc: What makes a window cleaning product genuinely eco-friendly in BC — and why it matters near Shuswap Lake. Learn what Wagon Windows uses and how to spot greenwashing.
h1: Eco-Friendly Window Cleaning Products in BC: What Actually Matters
intro: In British Columbia — and especially in the Shuswap watershed — the chemicals that run off your windows after a cleaning don't just disappear. They enter storm drains, reach salmon-bearing streams, and ultimately flow into Shuswap Lake. Choosing a window cleaning service that uses genuinely eco-friendly products isn't a luxury preference; for properties near the water, it's part of stewardship. Here's what to look for, what to avoid, and what Wagon Windows actually uses.

sections.h2: What Makes a Cleaning Product Genuinely Eco-Friendly
sections.body:
The term 'eco-friendly' has no legal definition in Canada, which means any company can print it on a label regardless of what's inside the bottle. Meaningful eco-friendly classification requires examining specific chemical properties rather than trusting marketing language.

The three criteria that actually matter are biodegradability, phosphate content, and aquatic toxicity. Biodegradability means the active cleaning compounds break down in the environment into non-toxic components within a defined timeframe — typically 28 days under OECD 301 test conditions. Non-biodegradable surfactants persist in waterways, bioaccumulate, and disrupt aquatic ecosystems.

Phosphates accelerate algae growth in lakes and rivers — a process called eutrophication. When algae blooms die and decompose, they consume dissolved oxygen, which kills fish and degrades aquatic habitat. Shuswap Lake has experienced algae bloom events, and any chemical contribution to phosphate loading in the watershed is a meaningful concern.

Aquatic toxicity is measured by LC50 — the concentration required to kill 50% of a test population of aquatic organisms. Products with low LC50 values (high toxicity) should never be used near surface drainage in lake-adjacent communities.
bullets: Biodegradable surfactants: breaks down within 28 days under OECD 301 criteria
Phosphate-free: no contribution to eutrophication in Shuswap Lake
Low aquatic toxicity: high LC50 threshold means low risk to aquatic life
No volatile organic compounds (VOCs) that off-gas near waterways
pH-neutral or near-neutral formulations preferred for sensitive surfaces

sections.h2: Why This Matters Specifically in the Shuswap
sections.body:
The Shuswap watershed is one of the most ecologically significant freshwater systems in British Columbia. Shuswap Lake supports sockeye salmon runs that are part of the Adams River system — one of the largest sockeye migrations in the world. The lake and its tributary streams are sensitive to chemical loading from residential and commercial runoff.

Properties along the Shuswap lakeshore, in Blind Bay, Sorrento, Scotch Creek, and along the Salmon River corridor drain directly or through shallow groundwater into this system. A window cleaning product with even moderate aquatic toxicity, applied repeatedly across dozens of lakefront homes during peak season, adds up to a measurable environmental load.

Beyond the ecological argument, there's a practical one: Shuswap Lake residents chose to live there because of the water. Using chemicals that degrade what makes the place valuable is simply self-defeating. The communities that depend on clean water for recreation, tourism, and property values have a direct stake in every product choice made on their shorelines.
sections.body2: BC's Environmental Management Act and federal Fisheries Act both prohibit the deposit of deleterious substances into waters frequented by fish. While enforcement at the individual household level is rare, professional window cleaning operations working near salmon-bearing waters are expected to use products that comply with these standards.

sections.h2: What Wagon Windows Uses and Why
sections.body:
Wagon Windows uses biodegradable, phosphate-free cleaning solutions formulated for professional window applications. Our primary cleaning agent uses plant-derived surfactants — specifically alcohol ethoxylate compounds derived from renewable plant sources — that are readily biodegradable and rated safe for aquatic environments at operational concentrations.

For pure water systems, we use deionized water that leaves no mineral residue and requires no chemical additives at all. Deionized or reverse-osmosis purified water is the purest possible rinsing medium — it dries spot-free because it has zero dissolved solids to deposit, and it introduces nothing to the runoff stream but water.

For hard water stain removal, we use citric acid-based treatments where possible. Citric acid is naturally occurring, readily biodegradable, and far less aggressive than the hydrofluoric or hydrochloric acid-based products that some operators use. On severe silica-bonded deposits, we escalate to the mildest effective chemistry and never use products that are not approved for use near waterways.

We avoid products containing phosphates, artificial fragrances, synthetic dyes, or surfactants that are not certified biodegradable. This is not a marketing decision — it's a values decision made by a company that operates in the Shuswap and intends to keep operating here.
bullets: Plant-derived surfactants — biodegradable, phosphate-free
Deionized/RO purified water rinse systems where applicable
Citric acid-based treatments for hard water stain removal
No products containing phosphates, synthetic dyes, or non-biodegradable surfactants

sections.h2: What Consumers Should Look For (and Greenwashing Red Flags)
sections.body:
When evaluating any cleaning product or service for genuine environmental claims, look for third-party certifications rather than self-declared labels. In Canada, EcoLogo (now UL ECOLOGO) is the most rigorous government-backed certification for cleaning products. Products bearing this mark have been independently tested and verified against documented environmental criteria.

European Ecolabel and EPA Safer Choice (US) are additional credible marks that appear on products available in Canada. These certifications require annual renewal and third-party audit, which self-declared labels do not.

Greenwashing red flags to watch for: vague language ('natural', 'plant-based', 'green formula') without certification; unverifiable claims about biodegradability without a timeframe or test standard; 'non-toxic to humans' claims that say nothing about aquatic toxicity; and products that list fragrance without disclosing what the fragrance compounds are (many synthetic fragrances are aquatically toxic).

For window cleaning specifically: if a company's product line doesn't include deionized water capability, they're using chemistry to compensate for hard tap water — and that chemistry runs off your windows. Ask what products they use and whether those products are certified. A company with nothing to hide will answer directly.
bullets: Look for: EcoLogo (UL ECOLOGO), EPA Safer Choice, or EU Ecolabel certification
Red flag: 'natural' or 'plant-based' without third-party certification
Red flag: biodegradability claims without a timeframe or test standard
Red flag: no aquatic toxicity data available on request
Red flag: fragrance listed as an ingredient without compound disclosure

faqs.q: Does eco-friendly window cleaning actually work as well as conventional products?
faqs.a: Yes — particularly when combined with deionized water systems, which leave zero residue and outperform conventional chemistry on streak-free results. Biodegradable plant-derived surfactants are effective at cutting oils and atmospheric grime. The only area where conventional chemistry has an edge is on severe hard water mineral bonding, and even there, citric acid treatments perform well on most deposits.

faqs.q: Is Shuswap Lake at risk from residential window cleaning runoff?
faqs.a: Individual events are low risk, but cumulative loading from hundreds of properties using phosphate-containing products over a season is a real concern. The Shuswap watershed has experienced algae bloom conditions historically, and phosphate reduction at every source is part of the management approach. Using phosphate-free, biodegradable products is a simple, zero-cost way for homeowners and service providers to do their part.

faqs.q: What's the difference between deionized water and regular tap water for window cleaning?
faqs.a: Tap water contains dissolved minerals — calcium, magnesium, silica — that deposit on glass as it evaporates, causing spots and streaks. Deionized (or reverse osmosis purified) water has these minerals removed, so it dries spotless without any chemical residue. It's also the purest possible rinse, meaning zero chemicals enter the runoff stream.

faqs.q: Can I verify what products my window cleaner uses?
faqs.a: Yes — ask for the product name or Safety Data Sheet (SDS). Every commercial cleaning product has an SDS that lists ingredients, biodegradability data, and aquatic toxicity ratings. A professional company should provide this without hesitation. If they can't name the product they're using, that's a meaningful red flag.

cta_text: Wagon Windows uses biodegradable, phosphate-free products safe for the Shuswap watershed. Book your clean at (403) 837-5092.
