meta_title: Cottonwood Pollen Season and Your Windows BC | Wagon Windows
meta_desc: Cottonwood pollen season in BC packs screens and films glass fast. Learn when it peaks in the Shuswap, what it does to windows, and the right timing to clean.
h1: Cottonwood Pollen and Your Windows: A BC Shuswap Guide
intro: Every spring, the Shuswap goes white. The cottonwood and black poplar trees that line the rivers, lakeshore, and residential areas across Salmon Arm, Chase, and Sorrento shed their seeds in a blizzard of white cotton that coats everything outside. For homeowners, this means screens packed solid, tracks filled with fibre, and a film on glass that bonds quickly and requires more than a rinse to remove. Here's what cottonwood does to your windows — and the precise timing that makes the difference between an easy clean and a difficult one.

sections.h2: Cottonwood Season in BC: When and Where
sections.body:
Cottonwood (Populus trichocarpa) and black poplar (Populus nigra) are native and widely planted throughout interior BC. They are among the most prolific seed-shedding trees in the province. In the Shuswap, shedding typically begins in the last week of May and peaks in the second and third week of June — though this varies by 1-2 weeks depending on spring temperature. Warm springs advance shedding; cool, wet springs delay it.

Shuswap communities most affected by cottonwood include Salmon Arm (particularly the lower townsite, Salmon River corridor, and lakeshore areas), Chase Creek valley, Sorrento (shoreline areas adjacent to Shuswap Lake), and Blind Bay. The cottonwood density along the Salmon River from Canoe to the delta is among the highest in the region. Properties within 300 metres of major cottonwood stands receive several times more cotton deposit than hillside or escarpment properties above the main valley.

In the Thompson-Okanagan broadly, Chase and Kamloops tend to peak slightly earlier than Salmon Arm due to lower elevation and warmer spring temperatures. Sicamous and the higher-elevation communities to the north typically shed 1-2 weeks later than Salmon Arm. This sequential shedding matters for service timing — Wagon Windows schedules accordingly.
bullets: Peak shedding: late May to late June in the Shuswap (second-third week of June most common)
Most affected communities: Salmon Arm lower townsite, Chase Creek, Sorrento shoreline, Blind Bay
Shedding advances in warm springs, delays in cool wet springs
Properties within 300m of major cottonwood stands receive significantly more cotton

sections.h2: What Cottonwood Actually Does to Windows and Screens
sections.body:
The fluffy white material is not the only product of cottonwood shedding — it's the most visible part. Understanding all three components helps explain why cottonwood creates a window problem that regular cleaning doesn't fully address.

The cotton (seed fibre): The white fibrous material packs mechanically into window screens, door screens, and window tracks. A screen facing a cottonwood tree can reach 30-50% blockage within a single week of peak shedding. This restricts airflow through open windows and, when wet by rain or morning dew, creates a compressed, dense mat that adheres to the screen mesh. Removing packed cotton from screens requires physical removal — a hose-off or manual brush — before standard cleaning.

The pollen (microscopic): Cottonwood releases true pollen 4-6 weeks before the cotton shedding, typically in April. This pollen is one of the most potent seasonal allergens in BC and deposits a fine yellow-brown layer on glass during the pollen phase. Most homeowners don't notice this phase as visibly as the cotton phase, but it leaves a thin film on glass that, when combined with the subsequent cotton-phase residue, creates a layered deposit.

The sap and terpene film: Less visible but most problematic for glass. Cottonwood buds release a sticky aromatic resin in spring — the source of the distinctive sweet smell. This resin, when distributed by wind and dew cycles, deposits a very thin but chemically active film on glass and painted surfaces. This film acts as a bonding agent for subsequent pollen and dust particles, and it cures over time from a tacky surface to a more permanent coating. Standard detergent cleaning removes recent deposition; cured cottonwood resin requires a degreasing step or citrus-based solvent.
bullets: Cotton fibre: packs screens mechanically — physical removal required before cleaning
Pollen (April phase): fine film on glass, precursor to the more visible cotton phase
Sap/terpene film: sticky resin that bonds subsequent deposits — cures if left >2 weeks
Combined effect: a three-layer deposit that's more than simple dust

sections.h2: Why Timing Your Post-Cottonwood Clean Matters
sections.body:
The single most important decision in cottonwood window management is when to clean. Clean too early — during active shedding — and you've wasted the job. Clean too late — three weeks after peak — and the terpene film has cured to the point where a standard clean won't fully clear it.

The target window is the first 7-14 days after shedding has fully stopped. In Salmon Arm, this typically means booking for the last week of June through the first week of July. This timing captures the terpene film while it's still in the tacky-but-soluble state, removes all cotton and pollen residue, and clears screens before the heat of summer hardens debris into the mesh.

How to tell when shedding has stopped: walk outside and look at your vehicle or any horizontal surface that has been outside for 24 hours without rain. If no new cotton is accumulating, shedding is complete or nearly complete. Don't wait for zero-cotton days — the tail of the shedding season is sparse, and waiting for absolute zero means losing your optimal treatment window.

If you missed the optimal window and it's been more than 3 weeks since shedding stopped, tell your window cleaner. Cured cottonwood film benefits from a pre-treatment soak with a light citrus degreaser before the standard clean. Doing a standard clean first and expecting the film to clear is one of the more common post-cottonwood service errors.
sections.body2: For rental properties or seasonal properties left unmonitored during cottonwood season, a late-June booking as a standing annual appointment is the safest default — regardless of monitoring. It captures the optimal window 80% of years.

sections.h2: DIY vs Professional: The Cottonwood Case
sections.body:
Cottonwood window cleaning is one of the situations where the DIY vs professional question has a clearer answer than usual. The cotton removal from screens and tracks is physically straightforward and well within DIY capability. A hose-off plus a stiff brush on screens, and a vacuum plus damp cloth on tracks, addresses the physical debris component effectively.

What DIY consistently struggles with is the glass film. The pollen-plus-terpene film on glass requires the right chemistry applied with proper squeegee technique to clear without leaving residue. Using a household glass cleaner (typically alcohol-based) on a cottonwood-filmed window will often distribute the terpene film rather than removing it — leaving a smeared haze rather than clarity. This is the most common homeowner complaint: 'I cleaned my windows but they still look dirty.'

Professional cleaning with a biodegradable detergent formulated for organic soil removal, applied with a saturating scrubber and cleared with a clean rubber squeegee in proper technique, will clear the cottonwood film in a single pass. For most Shuswap homeowners, the post-cottonwood clean is a once-a-year professional service call that saves 3-4 hours of DIY frustration and delivers a reliably clear result.
bullets: DIY handles well: screen cotton removal (hose + brush), track vacuum and wipe
DIY struggles with: glass film — wrong chemistry smears rather than removes terpene residue
Professional advantage: correct detergent chemistry + squeegee technique clears film in one pass
Timing matters more than who does it — book within 2 weeks of shedding end regardless

faqs.q: When is cottonwood pollen season over in Salmon Arm?
faqs.a: Peak shedding in Salmon Arm typically ends between June 15-25, though warm springs can advance this to early June. The visible cotton (fluffy white seeds) is the last phase of shedding. Once you see two consecutive dry days with no new cotton accumulating on outdoor surfaces, the season is functionally over and you're in the optimal cleaning window.

faqs.q: Will cottonwood pollen damage my windows if left too long?
faqs.a: The terpene film from cottonwood doesn't etch glass the way mineral deposits do — it won't cause permanent damage. But it will cure over several weeks into a more resistant coating that requires more aggressive chemistry to remove. On windows that are also receiving mineral spray (lakefront properties), the terpene film accelerates mineral bonding by providing an adhesion surface. In that combination, delayed cleaning has compounding effects.

faqs.q: Do screens need to be removed to clean them after cottonwood season?
faqs.a: Not necessarily. Moderate cotton accumulation on screens can be cleared with a firm hose-off at angle, followed by a soft brush scrub, with screens in place. Heavy accumulation — full blockage of the mesh — is better addressed with screens removed, soaked and brushed. If your screens haven't been removed and cleaned in 2+ years, cottonwood season is a good prompt to do so.

faqs.q: Does Wagon Windows clean during cottonwood season?
faqs.a: Yes — we accept bookings during active shedding for track, screen, and debris clearing, but we advise clients that glass cleaning scheduled during peak cotton shed should be understood as a preliminary clean that will need re-doing after shedding stops. We recommend post-shedding bookings for the glass clean that counts. Call (403) 837-5092 to discuss timing for your property.

cta_text: Book your post-cottonwood clean with Wagon Windows — the first 2 weeks of July fill fast. Call (403) 837-5092.

meta_title: Window Cleaning Armstrong BC: Agricultural Dust Guide
meta_desc: Armstrong and Spallumcheen Valley homes accumulate window grime faster than most BC communities. Learn why dairy and grain dust differs from lake minerals — and what to do about it.
h1: Window Cleaning in Armstrong, BC: The Agricultural Dust Problem
intro: Armstrong and the Spallumcheen Valley are different from Salmon Arm, Sicamous, or anywhere along the Shuswap lakeshore. There's no lake spray. The mineral deposit problem is different. Instead, there's a steady load of agricultural dust from dairy operations, grain fields, and the Okanagan pollen corridor that pushes northward through the valley each summer. Homes in Armstrong accumulate exterior grime at a rate that surprises most new residents — and requires a different approach than lake-area window care.

sections.h2: What Makes Armstrong's Dust Problem Different
sections.body:
The Spallumcheen Valley around Armstrong is BC's most significant dairy-farming area outside the Fraser Valley. The combination of large-scale dairy operations, grain and hay field cultivation, and the valley's orientation along the Okanagan wind corridor creates a persistent agricultural dust profile that affects home exteriors year-round.

Dairy dust is a mix of fine dried manure particulate, feed chaff, and bedding material (straw, sawdust) that becomes airborne during field operations, barn ventilation, and dry summer conditions. This particulate is organic and has a high protein and lipid content — meaning it doesn't just sit on glass like mineral dust; it can bond and develop biofilm if left long enough without cleaning. Homes within 1-2 km of active dairy operations and downwind during prevailing south and west winds are most affected.

Grain farming in the valley — primarily barley, wheat, and grass seed — adds a second layer of chaff and husk material during harvest (August–September). Harvest dust events in Armstrong can leave a visible yellow-brown film on south and west-facing windows within 48 hours. This is a coarser material than dairy dust but creates a textured surface on glass that traps subsequent fine particles.

The Okanagan pollen corridor contributes a third component from April through June — dry Okanagan air carries cottonwood, poplar, and grass pollen northward through the valley, adding an organic film layer on top of the agricultural dust base.
bullets: Dairy particulate: organic, protein-lipid content, bonds and develops biofilm
Grain harvest chaff: August–September, heavy deposits on south/west-facing glass
Okanagan pollen corridor: April–June, pollen film carried northward through the valley
Combined: three-layer organic deposit that accumulates faster than mineral-only environments

sections.h2: How Armstrong Grime Differs from Lake-Area Mineral Deposits
sections.body:
The key distinction between Armstrong's window grime and Shuswap lakefront mineral deposits is chemical composition and how each type of deposit must be removed.

Lake-area mineral deposits (calcium, silica, magnesium) are inorganic and bond to glass through crystal formation — they require acid-based chemistry (citric acid) to dissolve. They appear as white or translucent hazy scale. They are chemically stable and don't grow over time once deposited; they simply accumulate more layers.

Armstrong agricultural dust is primarily organic — it responds differently to cleaning chemistry. Alkaline or pH-neutral degreasing chemistry is more effective on organic deposits than the acid chemistry used for mineral scale. A mild alkaline surfactant (such as a plant-based detergent at higher dilution) cuts through the lipid and protein components of dairy dust more effectively than a citric acid rinse. Using hard water treatment chemistry (acid) on organic-dominant deposits can actually fix the organic layer more firmly to the glass in some conditions.

For Armstrong homes, the correct approach is: assess the dominant deposit type first. If the white haze runs off with a wet squeegee pass, it's organic dust — alkaline clean. If it remains after standard cleaning, visible as spots after drying, it's mineral — acid treatment. Many Armstrong properties have both, in which case a two-stage clean (alkaline first, acid second if indicated) is the right protocol.
sections.body2: The practical implication for booking: tell your window cleaner you're in Armstrong and that agricultural dust is likely the dominant load. A company that routes the same chemistry to every job regardless of location is not accounting for the material difference in what they're removing.

sections.h2: Cleaning Frequency for Armstrong Homes
sections.body:
Based on the agricultural dust profile, Armstrong homes typically require more frequent cleaning than urban Salmon Arm properties to maintain the same visual standard.

A year-round Armstrong residence with south or west-facing glass and moderate proximity to agricultural operations should plan on at minimum three cleans per year: spring (April–May, before grain planting dust), post-harvest (September), and either an additional summer clean during peak dust season or a pre-winter clean in November.

Homes directly adjacent to active dairy farms or along the major agricultural road corridors (Stepney Road, Rosedale Avenue area, Pleasant Valley Road agricultural corridor) may need quarterly cleaning to stay ahead of the biofilm formation threshold — the point at which organic deposits have been on glass long enough to begin supporting microbial growth. Once a biofilm establishes on glass, standard cleaning removes the surface layer but leaves the base layer, which reseeds rapidly. Catching deposits before this stage is significantly easier than treating established biofilm.

For Armstrong properties cleaned less than once per year, the first professional clean should include an assessment of the glass condition and a two-stage clean protocol. Setting a maintenance frequency afterward is substantially more economical than periodic deep-clean recovery from advanced accumulation.
bullets: Year-round Armstrong homes: minimum 3 cleans per year
Adjacent to dairy operations/ag corridors: quarterly
Key clean windows: April (pre-planting), September (post-harvest), November (pre-winter)
Biofilm threshold: organic deposits left >6-8 weeks may begin supporting microbial growth

sections.h2: Wagon Windows Serving Armstrong: Distance, Access, and the Interior Fair
sections.body:
Armstrong is approximately 45 minutes south of Wagon Windows' Salmon Arm base — a service distance we cover for residential and commercial properties throughout the Spallumcheen Valley. Armstrong is not a remote outpost for us; it's a regular service area, and we understand the specific conditions that Armstrong properties face.

For Armstrong commercial operations, the Interior Provincial Exhibition (IPE) fairgrounds and the commercial strip along Pleasant Valley Road represent a significant pre-fair cleaning window each September. The IPE draws major provincial attendance and is one of the highest-profile community events in the North Okanagan — commercial properties adjacent to the fairgrounds and on high-visibility routes benefit substantially from a pre-fair clean that accounts for the summer dust accumulation.

Wagon Windows accepts Armstrong bookings year-round. We schedule Armstrong-area runs to optimize travel efficiency, which means pricing for Armstrong is competitive and does not include punitive travel surcharges. For Armstrong homeowners who want to book alongside neighbors to reduce travel cost further, we offer group booking discounts when three or more addresses in the same neighborhood are scheduled on the same day. Call (403) 837-5092 to arrange.
bullets: Armstrong: 45 minutes from Salmon Arm base — regular service area
Pre-IPE commercial cleaning: September, targeted to fairground-adjacent businesses
Group booking discount: 3+ addresses same neighborhood, same day
No punitive travel surcharges for Armstrong and Spallumcheen

faqs.q: Does agricultural dust cause permanent damage to windows?
faqs.a: Organic agricultural dust does not etch glass the way mineral deposits do. However, if left long enough — particularly in warm, humid conditions — it can develop biofilm layers that are harder to remove than fresh deposits. On painted window frames, high-organic dust environments accelerate paint chalking and UV degradation. Regular cleaning prevents these longer-term issues.

faqs.q: How far from Armstrong do you serve?
faqs.a: Wagon Windows serves Armstrong, Spallumcheen, Enderby, and the surrounding North Okanagan communities as part of our regular service area. We also serve the Shuswap communities north toward Salmon Arm and Sicamous. For a specific address, call (403) 837-5092 to confirm service availability and pricing.

faqs.q: Is window cleaning different for farmhouses vs town homes in Armstrong?
faqs.a: Yes — farmhouses on working agricultural properties typically have a significantly heavier grime load, more complex rooflines (which affects ladder access), and may have agricultural chemical overspray on glass in addition to dust. We assess farm properties individually. Town homes in Armstrong receive the same service protocol as rural residential, with adjustments for the agricultural dust chemistry described above.

faqs.q: Do you clean gutters in Armstrong as well?
faqs.a: Yes. Armstrong properties benefit from gutter cleaning at least twice per year — the grain harvest season (September–October) loads gutters with chaff and organic material faster than most BC communities. We provide combined window and gutter cleaning in Armstrong, which reduces total visit cost and keeps both surfaces maintained on the same schedule.

cta_text: Wagon Windows serves Armstrong and the Spallumcheen Valley. Book your agricultural dust clean — call (403) 837-5092.

meta_title: Vacation Rental Window Cleaning Shuswap | Wagon Windows
meta_desc: Shuswap vacation rental owners: clean windows protect your reviews, justify premium rates, and are easy to schedule. Learn the best timing and how to bundle services.
h1: Vacation Rental Window Cleaning in the Shuswap: Why It Matters and When to Book
intro: A Shuswap vacation rental competes on photography, reviews, and the first-impression moment when guests walk through the door. Dirty windows undermine all three. For owners managing properties in Sorrento, Sicamous, Blind Bay, or Scotch Creek, window and gutter cleaning is not a maintenance chore — it's a guest experience investment that directly affects your ratings and your nightly rate. Here's how to think about it, when to schedule it, and how to do it efficiently.

sections.h2: Why Vacation Rentals Need Cleaner Windows Than Owner-Occupied Homes
sections.body:
Owner-occupied homeowners tolerate gradual window degradation because they adapt to it slowly. Vacation rental guests don't have that tolerance — they arrive with fresh eyes, high expectations calibrated by the listing photos, and an immediate readiness to notice anything that doesn't match the promised experience.

The guest experience begins the moment they pull into the driveway. For a lakefront Shuswap property, the view is the product — and the view is delivered through the windows. A guest arriving to cloudy, mineral-spotted glass on a lakefront property at Blind Bay or Sorrento immediately calibrates their expectations downward for everything else in the property. The psychological cost of that downward calibration is not recovered by fresh towels or a welcome gift.

The review impact is real and measurable. Cleanliness is consistently one of the top three rating factors on both Airbnb and VRBO. Reviewers who mention dirty windows in reviews use language that affects booking conversion — 'could be cleaner,' 'windows were a bit grimy,' 'didn't match the photos' — and that language lingers on your listing for months.

For premium-priced properties — lakefront homes on Shuswap, Blind Bay, or Scotch Creek listed at $400–$700/night — the threshold for visible imperfection is lower. Guests paying premium rates have premium expectations. A window clean costing $250–$350 that prevents one negative review or enables one additional booking at $450 has an obvious return.
bullets: Guests arrive with fresh eyes — they notice what residents have adapted to
First impression at driveway sets the tone for the entire stay
Cleanliness is a top-3 rating factor on Airbnb and VRBO
Premium-priced properties face higher guest expectation thresholds
Negative review language ('grimy,' 'didn't match photos') affects future booking conversion

sections.h2: Seasonal Timing: The Three Critical Windows
sections.body:
Shuswap vacation rental markets have distinct seasonal patterns that create three specific scheduling imperatives.

Pre-Victoria Day (late April to early May): The long weekend that opens the Shuswap summer season. Families arriving on the first warm long weekend of the year have been waiting through a BC winter for this moment. The property should be at its peak condition for this opening weekend. Book window and gutter cleaning in early-to-mid April to ensure completion before the rush.

Pre-Canada Day (mid-June): The peak of peak season. Canada Day weekend on the Shuswap is among the highest-demand booking periods of the year. If cottonwood shedding has occurred (it typically ends in the third week of June), a pre-Canada Day clean addresses the cotton film and sets the property up for the July high season. This is the most important clean of the year for most vacation rental owners.

Pre-Labour Day (late August): The final high-traffic weekend before the end of summer season. Properties that have been in continuous occupancy through July and August are showing six weeks of guest traffic and summer accumulation. A late-August refresh — even a quick clean — maintains the property's condition for the last high-occupancy weekend and the slower shoulder season that follows.
bullets: Pre-Victoria Day: April — open the season at peak condition
Pre-Canada Day: mid-June — most important clean, addresses cottonwood season
Pre-Labour Day: late August — refresh after continuous summer occupancy
Post-season: October — close the property clean for winter and next spring's listing photos

sections.h2: Key Shuswap Vacation Rental Markets: Where the Properties Are
sections.body:
The Shuswap vacation rental market is concentrated in a few specific communities that have a distinct character from the year-round residential base.

Sorrento and Blind Bay: The quietest and most resort-like of the Shuswap rental communities. Properties here skew toward family lakefront cottages and high-end lakehouse rentals. Mineral spray from Shuswap Lake is significant in both communities. Many properties are owned by Lower Mainland or Alberta families who are not present for the full season — making a reliable local service provider essential.

Sicamous: The houseboat capital of Canada, and a high-volume tourism destination. Sicamous rental properties serve both the houseboat-tourism crowd and the snowmobile-tourism crowd in winter. Properties here are often booked nearly continuously through summer and need reliable maintenance visits between turnovers. The proximity to the Shuswap and Mara Lake system creates similar mineral spray conditions to Blind Bay.

Scotch Creek and Celista: The north arm of the Shuswap Lake. More remote, higher premium properties, with a high proportion of large lakefront homes. Guest expectations at this end of the market are high. Distance from Salmon Arm means these bookings are batched efficiently with neighboring properties.

For all these communities, Wagon Windows provides service throughout the season. For vacation rental owners with multiple turnovers, we can work around your booking calendar — even tight turnaround windows between guests.
sections.body2: For property managers overseeing multiple Shuswap vacation rentals, Wagon Windows offers portfolio pricing. Managing three or more properties on a seasonal schedule qualifies for package rates. Call (403) 837-5092 to discuss.

sections.h2: Bundling Window and Gutter Cleaning for a Single Visit
sections.body:
For vacation rental owners operating at arms' length from their property, every service visit is a logistics event. Coordinating multiple vendors — window cleaner one week, gutter cleaner another — doubles the coordination overhead and often means two sets of travel costs.

Wagon Windows provides both window cleaning and gutter cleaning, which means a single visit can cover both. For a standard Shuswap vacation rental at Blind Bay or Sorrento, a combined window and gutter clean takes 2.5–4 hours. The logistical savings alone are worth bundling, but the condition alignment matters too: gutters cleaned of cottonwood and debris at the same time windows are cleaned keeps the property in a consistent state of maintenance rather than having windows clean but gutters overflowing.

For the pre-Canada Day service, the sequence is: gutters first (prevents debris from splashing onto freshly cleaned glass below), then windows. This single pre-season visit sets the property up for the peak booking period with both systems properly maintained.

Property managers and remote owners can authorize Wagon Windows to complete the service independently and receive a condition report and photos by email. We do this regularly for Lower Mainland and Alberta owners with Shuswap properties. Your property is maintained to standard without requiring a personal trip for a service visit.
bullets: Single visit covers both window and gutter cleaning — one coordination event
Sequence: gutters first, then windows (prevents splash-back on clean glass)
Remote owner authorization available — photos and condition report emailed
Portfolio pricing for 3+ properties managed by the same owner

faqs.q: How far in advance should I book pre-Canada Day window cleaning for a Shuswap rental?
faqs.a: Book in April or early May. The post-cottonwood window (late June) is the most in-demand period of the Wagon Windows schedule. Clients who book in spring get their preferred dates; clients who call in mid-June are working around what's left. For peak-season vacation rentals, early booking is the most important logistical step.

faqs.q: Can Wagon Windows work around a tight guest turnover schedule?
faqs.a: Yes — within reason. We can schedule arrival between checkout and check-in for properties with same-day turnovers, though we need a minimum 3-hour window for a full window and gutter clean on a standard-sized property. For same-day turnovers with short windows, windows-only service (1.5–2 hours) is a practical compromise. Discuss your booking calendar when arranging service.

faqs.q: Do Airbnb guests really notice window cleanliness?
faqs.a: Yes — particularly the first impression through the main window of a lakefront property, and particularly guests who are paying $300+ per night and have had their expectations set by professional listing photography. A review mentioning 'the views were incredible' (clean windows) versus 'the views would have been better if the windows were cleaner' represents a meaningful difference in social proof that accumulates over your review history.

faqs.q: What communities in the Shuswap does Wagon Windows serve for vacation rentals?
faqs.a: All of them — Salmon Arm, Blind Bay, Sorrento, Sicamous, Scotch Creek, Celista, Chase, Tappen, Canoe, and surrounding areas. Call (403) 837-5092 to discuss service frequency and seasonal scheduling for your rental property.

cta_text: Protect your Shuswap vacation rental reviews and nightly rate — book pre-season with Wagon Windows. Call (403) 837-5092.

meta_title: Lakefront Property Window Maintenance Guide | Wagon Windows
meta_desc: Complete lakefront window maintenance guide for Shuswap homeowners — lake spray staining, hard water treatment, seasonal cleans, mould prevention, and annual inspection checklist.
h1: The Complete Lakefront Property Window Maintenance Guide
intro: Lakefront property on Shuswap Lake is a significant investment — and the windows are both its most expressive feature and its most actively challenged component. Lake spray, mineral hardness, high humidity, north-facing mould, and the seasonal demands of an interior BC climate combine to create a maintenance challenge that generic window care advice simply doesn't address. This guide covers everything a Shuswap lakefront homeowner needs to know to protect their glass, frames, screens, and seals through every season.

sections.h2: Understanding Lake Spray Staining on Lakefront Glass
sections.body:
The primary window challenge on Shuswap lakefront properties is mineral deposit formation from lake spray. This is worth understanding in some depth because the mechanism determines both the treatment approach and the prevention strategy.

Shuswap Lake has a relatively high mineral content compared to the soft-water coastal lakes of BC. The dominant dissolved minerals are calcium bicarbonate (which precipitates as calcium carbonate when water evaporates), silica, and magnesium. When wind-driven spray evaporates on glass, these minerals are left behind as scale. The process is the same as the scale that forms in your kettle — just distributed across a vertical glass surface.

The rate of deposit formation depends on spray intensity (wind direction and speed), glass temperature (warmer glass evaporates spray faster, concentrating deposits), and sun exposure (south and west-facing glass in direct sun forms deposits faster). On a south-facing lakefront window during a typical August wind event, visible deposits can form within a single day.

Critically: early-stage deposits are much easier to remove than late-stage deposits. In the first weeks of formation, the mineral crystals are loosely bonded and respond to mild acid (dilute citric acid) and standard detergent. As crystals grow and integrate into the silica surface of the glass — a process that advances over months — they require stronger acid concentrations, longer dwell times, and professional technique to remove without scratching. Glass that has been left untreated for multiple seasons can reach the point where chemical cleaning alone is insufficient and physical restoration polishing is required.
bullets: Calcium bicarbonate, silica, magnesium — same as kettle scale, on glass
South/west-facing glass in direct sun forms deposits fastest
Early deposits: mild citric acid removes easily
Late-stage deposits: integrate into glass surface — require professional treatment
Multiple-season neglect: may require glass restoration polishing, not just cleaning

sections.h2: Seasonal Maintenance Calendar for Shuswap Lakefront
sections.body:
A four-season maintenance calendar is the most cost-effective approach for Shuswap lakefront homeowners. Each season addresses the dominant threat of that period before it compounds into the next.

Spring (April): The spring clean clears winter's legacy — condensation mineral rings on interior glass from the heating season, any mould that established on north-facing frames through the wet months, and the debris pressed against screens and tracks by snowmelt. The spring clean also provides the first opportunity to assess any seal degradation that occurred through freeze-thaw cycles. Check for interior fogging between panes on double or triple-pane units — this indicates seal failure that should be addressed before summer humidity makes the internal condensation permanent.

Summer (July): The mid-season mineral treatment clean. By July, south and west-facing glass has typically accumulated 3-4 months of mineral spray from spring wind events. The July clean with targeted hard water treatment prevents early-stage deposits from advancing to the bonded stage. This is the most chemically important clean of the year for lakefront glass. Priority surfaces: the main living room picture window, kitchen window facing the lake, and any glass that shows visible haze.

Fall (October): The post-season clean and inspection. This is the structural health check — beyond cleaning, it includes track and sill clearing, caulk inspection, screen removal or securing, and assessment of frame condition after the season's humidity cycles. Windows going into winter in clean condition resist debris intrusion during freeze-thaw better than dirty windows.
bullets: April: condensation rings, mould removal, seal inspection, track clearing
July: hard water mineral treatment on south/west-facing glass — most critical clean
October: full clean + structural inspection — caulk, seals, frames, track clearing
High-exposure properties: add a September check after storm season

sections.h2: Mould Prevention on North-Facing Glass in High-Humidity Lake Environments
sections.body:
North-facing windows on Shuswap lakefront properties face a different challenge than south-facing glass: instead of mineral deposits from sun-driven evaporation, they accumulate biological growth — mould, algae, and biofilm — fuelled by persistent shade and high humidity from the lake.

North-facing glass stays cooler and tends to have more condensation at frame edges, particularly during spring and fall transition seasons. This condensation, combined with organic material (cottonwood debris, airborne pollen, dust) that settles in shaded areas, creates ideal conditions for algae and surface mould. The first sign is usually a green-grey tint at frame edges or in the sill channel — if this appears, surface treatment is warranted immediately.

Prevention is straightforward: keep north-facing tracks and sills clear of debris (their moisture-trapping capacity is what enables biofilm formation), ensure exterior drainage away from the foundation doesn't create chronic splash-up on north-facing frames, and include north-facing glass in every cleaning rotation — not just the view-facing south and west glass.

For established mould or algae on frames and sills, a dilute hydrogen peroxide solution (3%) applied with dwell time and then rinsed is effective at the surface level and does not damage glass or vinyl frames. Heavy or recurring mould suggests a moisture source that should be investigated — caulk failure, drainage issue, or ventilation problem.
sections.body2: Wooden window frames on older Shuswap lakefront properties are particularly vulnerable to mould. Painted wood that has micro-cracking in the paint layer allows moisture ingress that standard surface treatment won't address. If mould recurs seasonally in the same location on a wood-framed window, the paint integrity needs assessment and likely repainting with a mould-inhibiting exterior paint.

sections.h2: Screen and Track Care in High-Humidity Lake Environments
sections.body:
Screens and tracks in a lakefront environment accumulate debris faster and are damaged by neglect more quickly than inland properties. The high humidity, organic material input (cottonwood, algae, lake debris), and frequent condensation cycles create conditions where standard annual cleaning is often insufficient.

Window tracks — the channels that guide sashes and receive screens — need to be cleared and lubricated at minimum twice per year on lakefront properties. Debris packing in tracks doesn't just restrict window operation; it creates a persistent moisture environment that accelerates corrosion in aluminum frames and can cause vinyl track channels to warp or develop permanent staining.

Insect screens on lakefront properties also have a higher-than-average mesh accumulation from aquatic insects (midges, mayflies, caddisflies are all prolific on Shuswap Lake in summer). The fine mesh becomes visually opaque before it becomes functionally blocked — which means light transmission through the glass is diminished by a grimy screen long before the homeowner notices restricted airflow. Clean screens also improve the view: a grimy mesh over a picture window is a daily minor degradation of the lakefront experience.

For season-specific screen management: screens on south and west-facing windows can typically stay in place year-round in the Shuswap climate. North-facing screens benefit from removal before winter — this reduces the debris accumulation that would otherwise mat against the north-facing glass and frame through the wet season.
bullets: Tracks: clear and lubricate twice per year — debris causes corrosion and frame damage
Screens: clean before mid-season and after cottonwood season at minimum
Aquatic insect accumulation on lake-facing screens reduces light transmission measurably
North-facing screens: remove before winter to prevent winter debris accumulation

sections.h2: Annual Inspection Checklist for Lakefront Windows
sections.body:
An annual condition review — separate from cleaning — is worth building into the maintenance routine for high-value lakefront properties. The following checklist covers the items that, if missed, become expensive.

Glass condition: Check south and west-facing glass for mineral etching (visible haze that persists after cleaning). Check north-facing glass for algae or biofilm tint. Check all panes for internal fogging (seal failure). Note any chips or micro-cracks, particularly at lower corners where thermal stress is highest.

Frame and sill condition: Check all exterior caulk joints for cracking, separation, or gaps. Check wood frames for paint chalking, micro-cracking, or soft spots (rot indicator). Check vinyl frames for warping at track channels or discolouration from mould. Check sill drainage holes are clear (tiny weep holes at the bottom of window frames — easily blocked by debris and the source of standing water in tracks).

Hardware condition: Check all window locks and operators for corrosion. Salt air and high humidity accelerate corrosion in standard window hardware; lakefront properties often need hardware lubrication or replacement sooner than manufacturer estimates. Check screen frame tension — loose screen frames allow screens to bow in wind and can pop out.

Wagon Windows can conduct the cleaning component of this checklist and flag condition items for homeowner review. A condition report is standard with every Wagon Windows service call. For full property assessments including structural considerations, a building inspector or window specialist should be engaged separately.
bullets: Glass: mineral etching, algae tint, internal fogging, chips and micro-cracks
Frames and sills: caulk integrity, paint/vinyl condition, weep hole clearance
Hardware: corrosion, operators, screen frame tension and seal
Seals: double/triple pane fog check — early detection avoids full unit replacement

faqs.q: How much does it cost to maintain lakefront windows professionally each year?
faqs.a: For a typical Shuswap lakefront home with standard window count (15-25 windows including large picture windows), a three-clean-per-year program runs $600–$1,200 annually, depending on property size, window count, and whether hard water treatment is required. This includes one mineral treatment session in summer. Compared to the cost of seal replacement ($400–$800 per unit) or glass etching restoration ($200–$600 per window), preventive maintenance is by far the lower-cost option.

faqs.q: How do I know if my lakefront window seals have failed?
faqs.a: Seal failure appears as fogging or condensation between the panes of a double or triple-pane unit — this fog is inside the unit and cannot be wiped away. It typically appears as a grey or milky haze in part of the pane. Mild fogging may only be visible in morning light; advanced seal failure shows as a persistent opaque patch. Once seals fail, the only remedy is unit replacement — cleaning cannot address internal fogging.

faqs.q: Is it safe to use vinegar on lakefront window mineral deposits?
faqs.a: Dilute white vinegar (50% water solution) works on early-stage light deposits with light touch. It is not effective on moderate to heavy mineral bonding, and using it with any abrasive material (even paper towel on tempered glass) risks micro-scratching that permanently traps minerals. For Shuswap lakefront glass with more than one season of untreated mineral accumulation, professional citric acid treatment with proper technique and rinsing is safer and more effective.

faqs.q: Does Wagon Windows provide condition reports with lakefront service calls?
faqs.a: Yes — every Wagon Windows service call includes a condition report noting anything observed that the homeowner should know about: mineral deposit severity, frame condition, caulk status, any glass damage. For remote owners or property managers, we provide photos of flagged items by email. This report comes at no additional charge and is how we build the kind of long-term relationship where clients call us every year without shopping around.

cta_text: Schedule your Shuswap lakefront property's annual window maintenance program with Wagon Windows. Call David at (403) 837-5092.

meta_title: Window Cleaning in Winter Salmon Arm — Worth It?
meta_desc: Is window cleaning worth it in Salmon Arm winters? Honest answer for homeowners and businesses, plus why a fall clean beats a winter one every time.
h1: Window Cleaning in Winter Salmon Arm: What Actually Makes Sense
intro: Every winter in Salmon Arm, we get asked the same question: can you clean windows when it's cold out? The honest answer is nuanced — sometimes yes, sometimes no, and sometimes the better move is to wait. Here's the real breakdown so you can make the right call for your home or business.

sections.h2: The Honest Answer: Winter Cleaning Depends on the Job
sections.body:
Window cleaning in sub-zero temperatures isn't impossible, but it's genuinely harder and the results are less reliable. The core problem is physics: water-based cleaning solutions freeze before the squeegee can remove them, leaving ice streaks that are worse than the dirt you started with. Professional-grade antifreeze additives (methanol-based products) push the safe working temperature down to around -10°C, but that's a ceiling, not a guarantee. Below that threshold, even treated solutions start failing.

For residential homes in Salmon Arm, most of the exterior window cleaning season winds down in late October or November. Once overnight lows are consistently near or below zero, the risk of freezing mid-clean — or of a window surface being too cold to release water — makes exterior cleaning unreliable. Homeowners who push for a December clean often end up with streaky glass that has to be redone in spring anyway.

That said, there are real exceptions. Interior-only window cleaning works year-round regardless of outside temperature. If you have a home office with grimy interior glass, or you want the inside of your sunroom windows cleaned before the holidays, a winter interior clean is perfectly sensible. The temperature constraint is purely an exterior problem.
bullets: Solutions can freeze on contact with cold glass below -5°C
Squeegee rubber stiffens in cold and loses its edge-seal effectiveness
Interior-only cleaning is unaffected by outside temperature
Professional antifreeze additives extend safe working range to approximately -10°C

sections.h2: When Winter Cleaning Makes Sense for Commercial Properties
sections.body:
Commercial properties in Salmon Arm often have a stronger case for winter window cleaning than residential homes. The calculation is different when dirty windows mean lost revenue. A restaurant on the main strip with fogged-up winter condensation and fingerprints on the door glass every morning has an ongoing image problem — and waiting until April to address it isn't viable.

For commercial clients, we evaluate winter jobs case by case. Heated interior vestibules, enclosed storefronts, or spaces where cleaning can happen during business hours (when interior heat warms the glass) are workable. Flat commercial windows with no sills for water to pool and refreeze are easier than complex residential window profiles. If the forecast shows a few days above 2°C, a commercial clean can be scheduled strategically.

Property managers who maintain multi-unit residential or commercial buildings sometimes schedule winter cleans for common-area interior glass — lobbies, stairwells, enclosed corridors — where the environment is fully controlled. These jobs proceed identically to a summer clean.

The businesses that benefit most from staying on a year-round schedule are those in the Shuswap's tourism and hospitality sector: hotels near Shuswap Lake, restaurants downtown, and retail shops that stay open for the ski and snowmobile crowd. If your business attracts visitors even in winter, your windows still need to represent you.
bullets: Restaurants and cafes: entry glass and interior windows are cleanable year-round
Hotels and lodges: lobbies and interior-facing glass not dependent on exterior temperature
Retail storefronts: can be scheduled during mild spells above 2°C
Multi-unit buildings: common-area interior glass is unaffected by season

sections.h2: What Temperature Does to Window Cleaning Equipment
sections.body:
Understanding why cold is a problem helps homeowners and business owners set realistic expectations. It's not just about the cleaning solution — cold affects the entire cleaning process from start to finish.

Squeegee rubber is the most temperature-sensitive component. At normal working temperatures, a good rubber blade is pliable and conforms perfectly to the glass surface, pulling water cleanly in a single pass. Below about 5°C, rubber hardens and loses its flex. The blade can't hug the glass evenly, and it leaves water trails that dry into streaks. Experienced cleaners warm their rubber before starting, but this is only a partial fix in sustained cold.

Cleaning solution behavior changes too. Even with antifreeze additives, very cold glass can cause solution to thicken and become difficult to spread evenly. If the glass temperature itself is below zero — which happens on north-facing windows after a cold night — the solution can freeze on contact before you've applied it to the whole pane. You end up chasing ice around the glass.

Water spots also form faster in cold. When solution dries before you can squeegee it, it leaves behind dissolved mineral deposits. Salmon Arm's water is moderately hard, which means there are enough dissolved minerals in untreated tap water to leave visible spots if drying outpaces cleaning. In warm weather this is manageable. In cold, it's a constant battle.
sections.body2: Finally, there's the physical safety element. Wet ladders, icy paths, and cold fingers all increase risk for the cleaner. Professional window cleaning companies factor safety into their winter decisions — not just results.

sections.h2: What Homeowners Should Do Instead: Preparing for Spring
sections.body:
If winter exterior cleaning isn't the right move for most Salmon Arm homes, what should homeowners actually do between November and March? The answer is: use the winter to set yourself up for a great spring clean.

First, check your window seals and frames while windows are closed up tight. Condensation forming between double-pane glass indicates a failed seal — a problem no amount of cleaning will fix. Better to identify this in winter and get a replacement quote, rather than discover it when you call for your spring clean.

Second, address interior glass that accumulated grime through fall. Cooking residue, pet nose-prints, and dust buildup on interior surfaces can be cleaned anytime. A DIY interior clean in January — or a professional interior-only visit — means your spring appointment covers only the exterior.

Third, plan your gutters for spring. Salmon Arm's fall leaf drop loads gutters heavily through October and November. By February, those leaves have composted into a dense sludge. Scheduling a combined window and gutter clean in early April — before the spring rains — is the single highest-leverage maintenance move for most homes. Book it in February before the calendar fills.

Finally, if you didn't get a fall clean before the freeze, don't panic. Winter grime — road salt mist, condensation residue, frost etching — is cleanable in spring with standard methods. It's not a permanent problem.
bullets: Check seals in winter — failed IGUs are a repair job, not a cleaning job
Clean interior glass any time of year without restriction
Book your spring clean (windows + gutters) in February before prime slots go
Winter grime from frost and salt cleans normally in spring — no panic needed

sections.h2: Why a Fall Clean Before the Freeze Is the Right Move
sections.body:
The single best thing a Salmon Arm homeowner can do is schedule a professional window clean in late September or October, before the first hard freeze. This is the move that avoids the winter dilemma entirely.

A good fall clean removes summer's accumulated pollen, wildfire smoke particulate (a real factor in the Shuswap), and insect residue before it has a chance to bake onto the glass through a winter of freeze-thaw cycles. Glass that goes into winter dirty comes out of winter with bonded mineral deposits and etched surfaces that are much harder to clean in spring. Glass that goes in clean just needs a quick spring freshen-up.

For gutters, the timing argument is even stronger. Cleaning gutters in late October — after the leaves have mostly fallen but before the ground freezes — means water flows freely all winter and you avoid the ice dam risk that comes with blocked gutters in a freeze-thaw climate like Salmon Arm's.

Wagon Windows typically books out the October window cleaning rush quickly. If you want a fall clean, calling in September puts you in the best position. Don't wait until November when availability gets tight and the weather is already borderline.
bullets: Fall clean removes wildfire smoke and pollen before it bonds to glass over winter
Clean windows entering winter = much easier spring maintenance clean
Late October gutter cleaning prevents ice dam risk through freeze-thaw cycles
September booking = best availability for October fall clean slots

faqs.q: Can Wagon Windows clean my windows in December in Salmon Arm?
faqs.a: Interior-only cleaning, yes — any time of year. Exterior cleaning in December depends on temperature. If we have a mild spell above 2°C and the forecast holds, we can schedule it. But we won't guarantee streak-free results when glass temperatures are near or below freezing, and we'll tell you honestly if we think you're better off waiting until spring.

faqs.q: What temperature is too cold for window cleaning?
faqs.a: Below -5°C exterior temperature, standard window cleaning solutions become unreliable. With antifreeze additives, we can work down to around -10°C in suitable conditions. Glass surface temperature matters as much as air temperature — north-facing windows on a cold morning can be below zero even when the air is a few degrees above.

faqs.q: My windows look terrible after winter. Is that permanent?
faqs.a: No. Winter grime — road spray, frost residue, condensation minerals — is normal and fully cleanable in spring with professional methods. If you see haze that won't clear after cleaning, that's likely a failed window seal (moisture between panes), which is a repair issue, not a cleaning issue.

faqs.q: When should I book my spring window clean in Salmon Arm?
faqs.a: February or early March for the best April availability. Spring cleaning season — when the weather reliably allows exterior work — typically opens in late March to early April in the Shuswap. The calendar fills fast. Booking two months out ensures you get a date that works before the prime May slots are gone.

cta_text: Book your fall clean before the rush — or get on the spring list now. Call David at (403) 837-5092.

meta_title: Post-Construction Window Cleaning Shuswap | New Build Specialist
meta_desc: New build windows need specialist cleaning to remove construction dust, paint overspray, and adhesive residue without scratching. What Shuswap homeowners need to know.
h1: Post-Construction Window Cleaning in the Shuswap: What New Build Windows Actually Need
intro: Moving into a new home in Salmon Arm or Armstrong is exciting — but the windows that look fine from a distance often tell a different story up close. Construction dust, paint overspray, sticker adhesive, and silicone residue are nearly universal on new build windows, and they require a very different approach than routine cleaning. Getting this first clean right sets the baseline for every clean that follows.

sections.h2: What's Actually on New Build Windows (It's Not Just Dust)
sections.body:
Post-construction contamination on windows is a mix of several distinct substances, each with different chemistry and removal requirements. Understanding what you're dealing with explains why a standard window cleaning isn't the right tool for the job.

Construction dust is the most visible issue, but it's also the most dangerous for glass. Drywall dust, concrete dust, and silica from cutting operations are all abrasive. The key risk isn't the dust itself — it's what happens when abrasive dust gets trapped under a squeegee or scrubber and is dragged across the glass surface. This is how new windows get scratched during improper post-construction cleaning. The pre-rinse step that professional cleaners use isn't ceremonial — it's the most important scratch-prevention measure on a new build job.

Paint overspray is the second major contaminant. Trade painters rarely mask every inch of glass, and spray drift reaches surprisingly far. On most new builds, there will be fine paint dots, splatter patterns, or edge runs on at least some windows. Water-based latex paint overspray, if caught fresh, rinses off. Older paint or oil-based products need controlled scraping with a fresh razor blade held at the correct angle. Done wrong, this scratches glass. Done right, it removes paint cleanly without a mark.

Label adhesive from manufacturer stickers is nearly universal on new windows. Some builders leave these on for protection during construction; others don't bother removing them before handover. The adhesive under labels is designed for permanence, and soap and water don't touch it. Proper adhesive removal requires a solvent step — most professionals use an orange-oil based product — before the standard clean.
bullets: Construction dust: abrasive, must be pre-rinsed before any scrubbing to prevent scratches
Paint overspray: requires controlled razor blade removal with proper technique
Label adhesive: solvent-based removal required before water-based cleaning
Silicone caulk residue: leftover from sealing frames, requires specific solvents
Grout haze: present on windows near tile work, needs acid-based or mechanical removal

sections.h2: Why New Build Windows Are Scratch-Risk Situations
sections.body:
New build windows occupy a risk category that experienced window cleaners treat differently from standard residential maintenance cleans. The scratch risk is genuinely elevated, for reasons that aren't always obvious.

Tempered glass — standard on most patio doors, side lites, and lower windows in modern construction — has a phenomenon called 'fabricating debris' or 'glass scratching syndrome.' During the manufacturing process, tiny glass particles can become embedded in the surface of tempered glass. These particles aren't visible to the naked eye, but when a scrubber or squeegee passes over them, they act like embedded sandpaper and create fine scratches. This is not a cleaning error — it's a manufacturing quality issue — but the scratches show up during the first clean, and untangling responsibility requires understanding this phenomenon.

The practical implication: professional post-construction cleaners should inspect tempered glass carefully, use soft scrubbers rather than steel wool or abrasive pads, and document the condition of each pane before starting. If fabricating debris is present, mechanical scrubbing on that glass creates scratches that aren't the cleaner's fault but are the cleaner's problem to manage.

Beyond fabricating debris, the combination of construction grit and new glass simply demands caution. A window that's been through a building site has been exposed to conditions that standard window cleaning technique doesn't account for. Pre-rinsing with low pressure to float off loose abrasive, then applying solution, then cleaning — rather than the standard scrub-immediately approach — is the right sequence for new builds.
sections.body2: Ask any professional you hire whether they've done post-construction cleans before and what their scratch-prevention protocol is. A vague answer is a red flag.

sections.h2: Salmon Arm and Armstrong New Build Context
sections.body:
The Shuswap region — particularly Salmon Arm and Armstrong — has seen sustained residential construction growth over the past several years. Housing demand from people relocating from the Lower Mainland and Alberta has driven new development in areas like Grandview Bench, North Broadview, and expanding subdivisions on the hillside above downtown Salmon Arm. Armstrong's agricultural land base and lower lot costs have made it a target for new residential projects as well.

This construction activity means post-construction window cleaning is not a rare specialty job in this region — it's a regular part of the seasonal workload. New build homeowners in the Shuswap face a few factors that make their situation specific:

Wildfire smoke season (typically July through September) can deposit a fine particulate film on windows during construction if the build spans a summer. This adds an additional layer to the post-construction contamination that isn't always present in other regions.

Hard water from municipal systems (and especially from well water on rural properties near Salmon Arm) means that any residual moisture on new windows during construction can leave mineral deposits on glass that's already contaminated with construction dust. These deposits mineralize quickly and require acid-based treatment to remove.

New builds that back onto agricultural land — common in the Armstrong area — face seasonal dust exposure from field operations: tilling, harvesting, and irrigation system spray all deposit material on windows during construction.
bullets: Grandview Bench and North Broadview among active residential development areas in Salmon Arm
Armstrong: growing new residential activity driven by land availability and lower lot costs
Wildfire smoke particulate is a regional factor for builds completed through summer
Hard water and agricultural dust compound post-construction contamination in this area

sections.h2: What to Ask Your Builder About Window Cleaning Responsibility
sections.body:
One of the most common sources of confusion for new build homeowners is who pays for the post-construction window clean. The answer depends entirely on what's in your purchase contract or builder's warranty, and it's worth clarifying before you close.

Many production builders include a 'construction clean' in their delivery package, but this is typically performed by a general cleaning crew, not window specialists. A construction clean usually means vacuuming, wiping surfaces, and mopping — not the specialist post-construction window cleaning we're describing here. If you ask your builder what their window cleaning process is and the answer is 'our cleaning crew handles it,' it's worth asking specifically whether they remove paint overspray, adhesive residue, and construction dust from glass with appropriate specialty products.

Some premium builders include a proper specialist post-construction window clean in the delivery package. If yours does, confirm that the company they use has post-construction experience — not just standard residential experience.

If the builder's clean doesn't include specialist window care, budget for it separately. On a typical Shuswap new build with 20-35 windows, a proper post-construction clean typically runs more than a standard residential maintenance clean, reflecting the additional time, specialized products, and higher skill level required. It's a one-time cost that's worth paying correctly rather than cutting corners on.

Get clarity on this in writing before the handover walkthrough. Walking through a new home and noting window condition in the deficiency list — if the builder's clean hasn't addressed the glass adequately — is your documentation for getting it resolved under warranty.
bullets: Ask specifically whether the builder's clean includes paint overspray and adhesive removal
General construction cleaning ≠ specialist post-construction window cleaning
Document window condition in the handover deficiency list if standard of clean is inadequate
Budget post-construction specialist cleaning as a separate line if builder doesn't include it

sections.h2: The First Clean Sets the Baseline for Everything After
sections.body:
Here's why the post-construction clean deserves more attention and budget than any subsequent routine clean: it establishes the starting point for the entire life of those windows.

If paint overspray isn't fully removed and gets left to harden, it becomes progressively harder to clean over time. The same is true for silicone residue and adhesive. These substances bond more strongly to glass with every freeze-thaw cycle and season of UV exposure. A post-construction clean done six months after move-in is significantly harder than one done at handover.

More importantly, any contamination that remains on glass after the first clean gets 'overcoated' by subsequent cleaning. Regular window cleaning — standard squeegee and solution work — doesn't penetrate bonded paint or silicone residue. Each subsequent clean makes the glass look cleaner in the moment, but the underlying contamination is still there, and eventually it becomes visible as a persistent haze or patch that won't clean out.

For homeowners investing in a new Shuswap build, the post-construction clean is the one appointment that protects the quality of the glass for the life of the home. Do it right, do it soon after handover, and do it with someone who knows the difference between a post-construction clean and a standard residential visit.

Wagon Windows handles post-construction window cleaning in Salmon Arm, Armstrong, and throughout the Shuswap. We assess each job before starting, document pane condition, and use the right product sequence for each contaminant type. Call David at (403) 837-5092 to discuss your new build.
bullets: Bonded paint and silicone harden over time and become progressively harder to remove
Standard routine cleaning does not penetrate bonded post-construction residue
Best timing: as soon as possible after builder handover, before first winter
A proper first clean protects glass quality for the life of the home

faqs.q: How is post-construction window cleaning different from regular window cleaning?
faqs.a: Post-construction cleaning addresses contaminants that standard window cleaning doesn't touch: paint overspray, label adhesive, silicone caulk residue, and abrasive construction dust. It requires solvent pre-treatment for adhesives, controlled razor blade work for paint, and a careful pre-rinse protocol to avoid dragging abrasive grit across glass surfaces. It typically takes longer and costs more than a routine maintenance clean.

faqs.q: Can I clean new build windows myself to save money?
faqs.a: For basic dust removal on easily accessible windows, yes. But paint overspray and adhesive removal involve chemistry and technique that's easy to get wrong. Improper razor blade use on tempered glass and the wrong solvent on certain window films or coatings can cause permanent damage. For anything beyond light dust, a specialist is worth the cost — especially given the investment in a new home.

faqs.q: My builder says the windows were cleaned. Why do they still look dirty?
faqs.a: Builder delivery cleans are usually general construction cleans, not specialist window cleaning. The crew wipes down surfaces but typically doesn't remove paint overspray, adhesive residue, or do the specialist pre-rinse work required for construction-contaminated glass. This is common across the industry. Request a specialist post-construction clean as a deficiency item, or budget for it independently.

faqs.q: How soon after moving in should I get a post-construction window clean?
faqs.a: As soon as possible after handover — ideally within the first month. Paint overspray and adhesive residue are easier to remove fresh. Waiting through a winter allows freeze-thaw cycles to bond contaminants more firmly to the glass. If your build was completed in fall, aim to get the clean done before the first hard freeze.

cta_text: Moving into a new build in Salmon Arm or Armstrong? Call David at (403) 837-5092 to book your post-construction clean before the first winter.
